rubric.foundational: A set of IT KPIs exists covering operational performance (availability, incident response) but lacks linkage to business outcomes or financial efficiency.
rubric.managed: IT KPIs span operational, financial, and business alignment dimensions. KPIs include cost efficiency, service quality, user satisfaction, and business value delivery. Targets are set and reviewed quarterly.
rubric.optimized: A balanced scorecard of IT KPIs is fully integrated with organizational performance management. KPIs are predictive as well as retrospective, with automated dashboards and cascading targets from strategy to operations.

items.text: To what extent are IT metrics normalized to enable fair comparison across time periods, organizational changes, and peer organizations?

rubric.ad_hoc: Metrics are reported as raw numbers without normalization. Year-over-year or peer comparisons are misleading due to differences in scope, scale, or methodology.
rubric.foundational: Some normalization is applied (e.g., per-user, per-device) but methods are inconsistent. Normalization does not account for differences in service scope, complexity, or quality.
rubric.managed: Metrics are consistently normalized using agreed-upon denominators and adjustment factors. Normalization methodology is documented, enabling meaningful trend analysis and peer comparison.
rubric.optimized: Advanced normalization accounts for complexity, service scope, quality, and organizational context. Statistical methods ensure fair comparisons. Normalization models are regularly validated and refined.

items.text: How well does the organization connect IT cost metrics to service quality and business outcome metrics?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cost metrics and quality/outcome metrics are tracked separately with no connection. Decisions to cut costs do not consider quality impacts, and quality investments are not evaluated for cost efficiency.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that cost and quality are related, but no formal linkage or analysis connects the two. Cost reduction and quality improvement are managed as separate initiatives.
rubric.managed: Cost-quality relationships are analyzed for major services (e.g., correlation between infrastructure spending and availability). Trade-off analysis informs decisions about cost optimization.
rubric.optimized: Integrated cost-quality-outcome models quantify the relationship between spending levels, service quality, and business outcomes. Optimization decisions are based on maximizing value (quality per dollar) rather than minimizing cost alone.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use IT metrics to support data-driven decision-making by agency leadership?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT metrics are not available to agency leadership in a usable format. Decisions about IT are made based on anecdotes, vendor presentations, or crisis response rather than data.
rubric.foundational: Basic IT metrics (budget status, major project updates) are provided to leadership periodically, but metrics are not actionable and do not support specific decision needs.
rubric.managed: A curated set of IT metrics is designed for leadership decision-making, covering cost efficiency, service performance, investment health, and risk posture. Metrics are presented with context and recommendations.
rubric.optimized: Executive IT dashboards provide real-time, interactive access to decision-relevant metrics. Predictive indicators alert leadership to emerging issues. Drill-down capability enables leaders to explore root causes. Metrics are directly linked to strategic objectives and mission outcomes.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use benchmarking data to set quantitative performance targets for IT service delivery?

rubric.ad_hoc: No performance targets are derived from benchmarking data. IT service performance expectations are based on historical baselines or vendor promises without external reference points.
rubric.foundational: Benchmarking data occasionally informs target-setting for a few visible metrics, but the practice is not systematic. Targets may reference industry averages without accounting for organizational context.
rubric.managed: Benchmarking data systematically informs performance targets across major IT service areas. Targets account for organizational maturity, peer group characteristics, and improvement trajectory. Targets are reviewed and updated annually.
rubric.optimized: Performance targets are dynamically calibrated using real-time benchmarking data from multiple sources. Stretch targets are set at top-quartile peer performance, and continuous improvement goals are embedded in service-level agreements.

items.text: To what extent does the organization participate in formal IT benchmarking studies or peer comparisons?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization does not participate in any benchmarking studies or peer comparisons. IT leadership has no external reference points for evaluating cost or performance levels.
rubric.foundational: Occasional participation in benchmarking surveys or informal peer discussions occurs, but results are not systematically analyzed or acted upon.
rubric.managed: The organization regularly participates in recognized benchmarking programs (e.g., TBM Council benchmarks, Gartner IT Key Metrics). Results are analyzed, shared with leadership, and used to identify improvement areas.
rubric.optimized: Benchmarking is a continuous practice with multiple data sources and peer groups. The organization contributes to and leads benchmarking communities. Benchmark results directly drive strategic planning and investment decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization select and use appropriate peer groups for benchmarking comparisons?

rubric.ad_hoc: No peer group selection process exists. Any available comparison data is used without regard for relevance (e.g., comparing a federal agency to a private-sector retailer).
rubric.foundational: Peer groups are selected based on basic criteria (e.g., sector, size) but may not account for important differences in mission complexity, geographic distribution, or security requirements.
rubric.managed: Peer groups are thoughtfully selected based on multiple relevant criteria including sector, size, complexity, mission profile, and security posture. Multiple peer groups may be used for different metrics.
rubric.optimized: Peer group selection is rigorous and regularly reviewed. Custom peer groups are constructed for specific metrics. Results are adjusted for known differences, and outlier analysis identifies both improvement opportunities and unique strengths.

items.text: How well does the organization act on benchmarking results to drive cost optimization and performance improvement?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benchmarking results, when available, are reviewed but do not lead to concrete action. Results may be dismissed as not applicable or shared without follow-up.
rubric.foundational: Benchmarking results occasionally inspire improvement initiatives, but the connection between benchmark findings and specific actions is loose. No formal process links benchmarks to action plans.
rubric.managed: Benchmarking results are systematically analyzed to identify gaps and opportunities. Improvement plans are developed for areas where performance significantly lags peers, with measurable targets and timelines.
rubric.optimized: A closed-loop process connects benchmarking results to strategic initiatives, tracks improvement progress, and validates results in subsequent benchmark cycles. Benchmarking insights are embedded in annual planning and continuous improvement.

items.text: To what extent does the organization contribute to government-wide benchmarking data sharing and collaborative improvement efforts?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization does not participate in government-wide benchmarking or data sharing initiatives. No contribution to collective improvement efforts occurs.
rubric.foundational: Occasional participation in government-wide surveys or studies provides data to community efforts, but engagement is passive and sporadic.
rubric.managed: Active participation in government-wide benchmarking programs includes regular data submissions, engagement in working groups, and sharing of practices. The organization uses collective data to inform its own improvement efforts.
rubric.optimized: The organization is a leader in government-wide benchmarking collaboration, contributing data, methodology expertise, and best practices. It champions cross-agency improvement initiatives and helps establish benchmarking standards for the government IT community.

items.text: To what extent does the organization participate in formal benchmarking consortia or peer exchange programs with other federal agencies?

rubric.ad_hoc: No participation in benchmarking consortia or peer exchanges. The organization operates in isolation with no structured mechanism for comparing practices with peer agencies.
rubric.foundational: Informal peer comparisons occur through professional networks or conferences, but the organization does not participate in formal benchmarking programs or data-sharing agreements.
rubric.managed: The organization participates in at least one formal benchmarking program (e.g., TBM Council, industry analyst benchmarking service) and regularly exchanges data with peer agencies through structured forums.
rubric.optimized: Active participation in multiple benchmarking consortia with reciprocal data-sharing agreements. The organization leads or co-leads peer exchange initiatives and contributes to government-wide benchmarking databases maintained by GSA or OMB.

items.text: How effectively does the organization perform trend analysis on IT cost and performance metrics over time?

rubric.ad_hoc: No trend analysis is performed. Metrics are point-in-time snapshots with no historical context. It is not possible to determine whether costs or performance are improving or degrading.
rubric.foundational: Basic trend data is available for a few key metrics (e.g., total IT spend over 3 years), but analysis is limited to simple charts without investigation of drivers.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive trend analysis is performed for all major metrics, covering 3-5 years of history. Trend drivers are identified and investigated (e.g., cost increases attributed to cloud migration, staffing changes, or demand growth).
rubric.optimized: Advanced trend analysis uses statistical methods to identify patterns, forecast future performance, and distinguish signal from noise. Leading indicators provide early warning of emerging trends.

items.text: To what degree does the organization maintain a continuous improvement program for IT cost efficiency?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cost optimization is reactive, occurring only in response to budget cuts or crises. There is no ongoing program to systematically identify and pursue efficiency improvements.
rubric.foundational: Periodic cost optimization exercises occur (e.g., annual contract renegotiation, infrastructure right-sizing) but are not part of a sustained program with dedicated resources.
rubric.managed: A formal continuous improvement program exists with dedicated resources, a pipeline of optimization opportunities, and regular reporting on realized savings. Opportunities are prioritized by impact and feasibility.
rubric.optimized: Continuous improvement is embedded in IT culture and operations. Automated tools identify optimization opportunities. Savings are tracked, validated, and reinvested strategically. The program has executive sponsorship and cross-functional participation.

items.text: How well does the organization track and validate realized savings from IT optimization initiatives?

rubric.ad_hoc: Savings from optimization efforts are not tracked. Claimed savings are anecdotal and cannot be verified against financial actuals. There is no baseline methodology.
rubric.foundational: Savings are estimated at initiative inception but not tracked through implementation. Validation against financial actuals is rare, and the difference between cost avoidance and hard savings is unclear.
rubric.managed: A formal savings tracking methodology exists with defined baselines, measurement periods, and validation against financial actuals. Hard savings, soft savings, and cost avoidance are distinguished and reported separately.
rubric.optimized: Automated savings tracking integrates with financial systems to continuously validate realized savings. A savings library catalogues validated opportunities by category. Historical accuracy of savings estimates informs future projections.

items.text: How well does the organization track and analyze multi-year trends in its IT spending and performance benchmarks to identify improvement trajectories?

rubric.ad_hoc: No trend analysis of benchmarking data is performed. Each benchmarking exercise is treated as a point-in-time snapshot without historical context.
rubric.foundational: Year-over-year comparisons are made for a few key metrics, but systematic multi-year trend analysis is not performed. It is unclear whether the organization is improving, declining, or stagnating relative to peers.
rubric.managed: Multi-year trend analysis is performed for all benchmarked metrics, showing improvement trajectories relative to peer group performance. Trend data informs investment priorities and is included in strategic planning documents.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive trend analytics include predictive modeling of future performance trajectories. Trend analysis is automated and integrated into executive dashboards. The organization uses trends to proactively adjust strategies before performance gaps widen.

items.text: How effectively does the organization benchmark IT costs and performance against other federal or state government agencies?

rubric.ad_hoc: No government-specific benchmarking is performed. The organization has no reference points for how its IT spending compares to peer agencies.
rubric.foundational: Informal comparisons with peer agencies occur through communities of practice or published data (e.g., IT Dashboard), but structured benchmarking is not performed.
rubric.managed: Structured benchmarking with peer government agencies is conducted using standardized methodologies. The organization participates in government benchmarking programs (e.g., TBM Council government cohort, ACT-IAC studies).
rubric.optimized: The organization leads government benchmarking initiatives, contributing data and methodology expertise. Multi-year trend analysis against peer agencies drives strategic planning. Results are shared with oversight bodies to demonstrate stewardship.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use FITARA scorecard metrics as benchmarks for IT management improvement?

rubric.ad_hoc: FITARA scorecard results are not used for benchmarking or improvement planning. Scorecard submissions are treated as compliance exercises rather than management tools.
rubric.foundational: FITARA scorecard results are reviewed when published, but no systematic action plans are developed to improve scores. Results are not compared against peer agencies.
rubric.managed: FITARA scorecard categories are actively managed with improvement plans for each metric area. Results are benchmarked against peer agencies, and trends are tracked across scoring cycles.
rubric.optimized: FITARA metrics are fully integrated into IT management practice, not just compliance. The organization uses scorecard results proactively to drive management excellence, shares best practices across government, and consistently achieves top-tier scores.

items.text: How well does the organization benchmark the cost-effectiveness of its IT workforce against government and industry peers?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT workforce costs are not benchmarked. The organization has no external reference for whether its blended rates, staff-to-user ratios, or contractor mix are competitive.
rubric.foundational: General awareness of government pay scales and contractor rate benchmarks exists, but systematic comparison of workforce cost-effectiveness metrics is not performed.
rubric.managed: IT workforce metrics (blended rates, staff ratios, contractor percentages, skill premiums) are regularly benchmarked against government and industry peers using recognized data sources.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive workforce benchmarking drives strategic workforce decisions. Analytics compare total workforce cost-effectiveness including productivity metrics. Results inform workforce planning, sourcing strategy, and compensation competitiveness assessments.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use IT Dashboard data and FITARA scorecard results as benchmarks for improving its own IT management practices?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT Dashboard data and FITARA scorecard results are treated as compliance artifacts rather than benchmarking tools. No effort is made to compare the organization's scores with peer agencies.
rubric.foundational: FITARA scorecard grades are reviewed when released, and IT Dashboard data is occasionally referenced, but no systematic benchmarking against peer agencies occurs. Improvement actions are not data-driven.
rubric.managed: IT Dashboard metrics and FITARA scorecard criteria are actively benchmarked against peer agencies. Gap analysis identifies specific areas for improvement, and action plans are developed to address lower-performing metrics.
rubric.optimized: The organization uses IT Dashboard and FITARA scorecard data as core benchmarking inputs, supplemented with additional peer agency data. A continuous improvement program tracks progress toward top-tier FITARA grades across all scorecard categories.

items.text: How effectively does the organization benchmark IT value delivery and business outcomes, not just cost metrics?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benchmarking focuses exclusively on cost metrics (e.g., IT spending as a percentage of revenue or budget). No benchmarking of value delivery or business outcomes is performed.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that value benchmarking is important, but the organization lacks methodologies or data to benchmark outcomes beyond cost. Occasional satisfaction surveys provide limited insight.
rubric.managed: Value benchmarking includes metrics such as user satisfaction, business process cycle time improvement, digital service adoption rates, and mission outcome contribution alongside cost metrics.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive value benchmarking uses balanced scorecards covering cost efficiency, service quality, innovation velocity, user experience, and mission impact. The organization contributes to evolving government value measurement frameworks.

items.text: To what extent does the organization benchmark its IT service delivery speed and agility against peers?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service delivery speed is not measured or benchmarked. The organization has no data on how long it takes to deliver common IT services compared to peers.
rubric.foundational: Delivery times for some services (e.g., endpoint provisioning, application deployment) are tracked informally, but not benchmarked against peers or industry standards.
rubric.managed: Key service delivery metrics (time to provision, change lead time, deployment frequency, incident resolution time) are tracked and benchmarked against government and industry peers.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive agility benchmarking covers the full service delivery lifecycle. The organization uses benchmarks to set aggressive improvement targets and tracks progress continuously. Results inform process improvement and automation investments.

items.text: How well does the organization benchmark its IT innovation and modernization investment levels against peers?

rubric.ad_hoc: Innovation and modernization spending are not measured or benchmarked. The organization cannot quantify its investment in new capabilities versus sustaining legacy systems.
rubric.foundational: Innovation spending is estimated at a high level but not systematically tracked or benchmarked. The organization is aware that peers may invest more in modernization.
rubric.managed: Innovation and modernization spending is tracked as a percentage of total IT budget and benchmarked against peer agencies. Portfolio analysis identifies opportunities to shift spending toward transformation.
rubric.optimized: Innovation investment benchmarking is comprehensive, covering spending levels, portfolio mix, and outcomes achieved. The organization actively manages its innovation portfolio to achieve top-quartile investment levels and demonstrable mission improvements.

items.text: How well does the organization benchmark its IT investment outcomes (mission value delivered) in addition to cost and operational efficiency metrics?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benchmarking focuses exclusively on cost metrics. No effort is made to benchmark the mission value or business outcomes delivered by IT investments.
rubric.foundational: Some qualitative comparisons of IT value delivery are made through case studies or peer discussions, but formal outcome-based benchmarking is not performed.
rubric.managed: The organization benchmarks IT investment outcomes using mission-relevant metrics (e.g., citizen service delivery times, processing accuracy, employee productivity gains). Value benchmarking is included alongside cost benchmarking.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive value benchmarking compares mission outcomes, citizen experience, and operational effectiveness across peer agencies. Value metrics are weighted equally with cost metrics in overall benchmarking assessments and inform investment prioritization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization collect, validate, and maintain data needed for meaningful benchmarking?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benchmarking data is not systematically collected. When benchmarking is attempted, data gathering is a major obstacle requiring weeks of manual effort.
rubric.foundational: Data for key benchmarking metrics is collected but may be inconsistent in methodology or timing. Data validation is limited, raising questions about comparability.
rubric.managed: A systematic data collection process produces consistent, validated benchmarking data on a regular cadence. Data definitions and collection methodologies are documented and aligned with benchmarking program requirements.
rubric.optimized: Automated data collection feeds benchmarking databases continuously. Data quality is monitored and maintained through automated validation rules. The organization can produce benchmark-ready data on demand for any metric.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use benchmarking tools and platforms to automate comparison and analysis?

rubric.ad_hoc: No benchmarking tools are used. Comparisons are performed manually using spreadsheets, if they are performed at all.
rubric.foundational: Basic spreadsheet-based templates are used for benchmarking analysis. Data from external benchmarking programs is imported manually for comparison.
rubric.managed: Dedicated benchmarking tools or modules within TBM platforms automate data collection, normalization, and comparison. Interactive dashboards display benchmarking results with drill-down capability.
rubric.optimized: Advanced benchmarking platforms provide automated, continuous comparison with dynamically updated peer data. Predictive analytics identify emerging performance gaps. Integration with planning tools enables direct action on benchmarking insights.

items.text: How well does the organization leverage publicly available government IT data (e.g., IT Dashboard, USASpending, SAM.gov) for benchmarking purposes?

rubric.ad_hoc: Publicly available government IT data is not used for benchmarking or analysis. IT leadership may not be aware of available data sources.
rubric.foundational: Some publicly available data is reviewed occasionally (e.g., IT Dashboard spending data for peer agencies), but no systematic analysis is performed.
rubric.managed: Publicly available government IT data is regularly analyzed to benchmark spending, contract costs, and service levels. Data from multiple sources is correlated to build comprehensive peer profiles.
rubric.optimized: The organization has automated processes to ingest, normalize, and analyze publicly available government IT data. Insights from public data complement formal benchmarking programs and inform strategic planning, acquisition strategy, and congressional communications.

items.text: How effectively does the organization leverage automated tools and platforms to streamline benchmarking data collection, normalization, and analysis?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benchmarking data is collected manually through spreadsheets and ad hoc surveys. Data normalization is inconsistent, making valid comparisons difficult and time-consuming.
rubric.foundational: Some automation exists for benchmarking data collection (e.g., exports from financial systems), but normalization and analysis remain manual. Tools are not integrated with benchmarking data sources.
rubric.managed: Automated tools support benchmarking data collection and normalization from multiple internal systems. Analysis templates standardize comparisons and produce consistent outputs for leadership review.
rubric.optimized: Integrated benchmarking platforms automatically ingest data from financial, operational, and service management systems. Normalized benchmarks are available on-demand through self-service dashboards with drill-down and peer comparison capabilities.

items.text: How effectively does the organization assess its own TBM maturity and develop improvement roadmaps?

rubric.ad_hoc: No TBM maturity assessment has been conducted. The organization has no baseline understanding of its TBM capabilities or a plan for improvement.
rubric.foundational: An informal sense of TBM maturity exists, but no structured assessment has been performed. Improvement efforts are opportunistic rather than planned.
rubric.managed: Formal TBM maturity assessments are conducted periodically using recognized frameworks. Results identify specific capability gaps and inform prioritized improvement roadmaps with milestones and resource requirements.
rubric.optimized: Continuous TBM maturity management includes regular assessment, benchmarking against peer agencies, and a living improvement roadmap. Maturity targets are set, tracked, and reported to leadership. The organization demonstrates measurable maturity advancement over time.

items.text: To what extent does the organization benchmark its TBM tool and process capabilities against leading practices?

rubric.ad_hoc: TBM tools and processes are not benchmarked. The organization does not know how its TBM capabilities compare to peers or to recognized maturity models.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of TBM leading practices exists through conference attendance or vendor interactions, but no systematic comparison of the organization's capabilities to leading practices is performed.
rubric.managed: TBM tool and process capabilities are benchmarked against leading practices and peer organizations. Gaps are documented, and improvement investments are justified through benchmark comparisons.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive benchmarking of TBM capabilities covers tools, processes, data quality, organizational structure, and business impact. The organization participates in TBM community knowledge sharing and is recognized for specific areas of excellence.

items.text: How well does the organization measure and demonstrate the business impact of its TBM program?

rubric.ad_hoc: The business impact of TBM is not measured. TBM is perceived as an IT finance reporting exercise rather than a driver of business value.
rubric.foundational: Anecdotal examples of TBM-enabled decisions exist, but systematic measurement of TBM program impact (e.g., savings identified, decisions improved, stakeholder satisfaction) is not performed.
rubric.managed: TBM program impact is measured through defined metrics including savings identified, optimization actions taken, decision quality improvements, and stakeholder satisfaction. Program ROI is calculated and reported.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive TBM program value management tracks the full impact of TBM capabilities on organizational decision-making, cost optimization, and mission delivery. The TBM program has demonstrated, quantified value that justifies ongoing investment and executive sponsorship.

items.text: How comprehensive and well-defined is the organization's IT service catalog?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal IT service catalog exists. Services are described informally and inconsistently. Users and business stakeholders cannot easily determine what IT services are available.
rubric.foundational: A basic service catalog lists major IT services but lacks consistent detail on service descriptions, owners, SLAs, or costs. The catalog may be incomplete or outdated.
rubric.managed: A comprehensive service catalog documents all IT services with standardized attributes including description, owner, SLAs, costs, and dependencies. The catalog is actively maintained and accessible to all stakeholders.
rubric.optimized: The service catalog is a dynamic, system-of-record for IT services, integrated with ITSM, financial, and demand management systems. Self-service capabilities allow users to browse, request, and track services. Lifecycle management ensures ongoing accuracy.

items.text: To what extent are IT services defined in business-relevant terms that non-technical stakeholders can understand?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT services are described in purely technical terms (e.g., 'VMware hosting,' 'Exchange administration'). Business stakeholders cannot relate service offerings to their work or missions.
rubric.foundational: Some services are described in business terms, but many remain technically oriented. No consistent methodology exists for translating technical capabilities into business service descriptions.
rubric.managed: IT services are consistently defined in business terms, describing the business capability or outcome they enable. Technical components are mapped to business services but not exposed to business users.
rubric.optimized: The service portfolio is organized around business capabilities and outcomes. Business stakeholders co-own service definitions. Service descriptions clearly articulate value propositions and are regularly validated with business consumers.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage the IT service lifecycle (pipeline, catalog, retired services)?

rubric.ad_hoc: No service lifecycle management exists. New services are introduced without formal process, and retired services may continue to consume resources because decommission is not tracked.
rubric.foundational: New services go through a basic approval process, but lifecycle management is limited. The status of services (planned, active, retiring) is not consistently tracked.
rubric.managed: A formal service lifecycle process governs introduction, modification, and retirement of services. Each service has a defined lifecycle stage, and transitions are managed through a review process.
rubric.optimized: Service lifecycle management is integrated with portfolio governance, demand management, and financial planning. Automated workflows manage transitions. Portfolio reviews regularly assess service viability and consolidation opportunities.

items.text: How well does the organization map IT services to the underlying technology components (applications, infrastructure, vendors) that deliver them?

rubric.ad_hoc: No mapping exists between business-facing IT services and underlying technology components. It is not possible to determine which infrastructure, applications, or vendors support a given service.
rubric.foundational: High-level mappings exist for some critical services but are maintained manually and may be incomplete or outdated. Dependencies are not well understood.
rubric.managed: Service-to-technology mappings are comprehensive and maintained in a CMDB or service mapping tool. Mappings include applications, infrastructure, vendors, and personnel dependencies.
rubric.optimized: Automated discovery and dependency mapping continuously maintain accurate service-to-technology relationships. Impact analysis can rapidly determine the blast radius of component failures or changes.

items.text: How well does the organization maintain and enforce service ownership and accountability across the IT service catalog?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service ownership is not formally defined. No individual or team is accountable for the end-to-end delivery, cost, and quality of specific IT services.
rubric.foundational: Service owners are designated for some major services, but accountability is unclear and ownership may be nominal. No performance expectations are tied to service ownership roles.
rubric.managed: Every cataloged service has a designated owner with documented accountability for service performance, cost management, lifecycle decisions, and stakeholder satisfaction. Service owners participate in portfolio governance.
rubric.optimized: Service ownership is a mature practice with empowered owners who manage services as products. Owners have P&L-like accountability, make investment decisions within governance guardrails, and drive continuous improvement. Service ownership is recognized as a career path.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain accurate service dependencies and relationship mapping within its IT service catalog?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service dependencies are not documented. When a service experiences an outage or change, the downstream impacts on other services and business processes are unknown until they manifest.
rubric.foundational: Some critical service dependencies are documented informally, but the mapping is incomplete and not maintained. Impact analysis during changes or incidents relies on institutional knowledge of key personnel.
rubric.managed: Service dependencies are systematically mapped and maintained in the service catalog or a CMDB. Dependency information supports change impact analysis, incident management, and business continuity planning.
rubric.optimized: Service dependency maps are automated, continuously validated against actual traffic and integration patterns, and integrated with monitoring tools. Real-time dependency visualization supports rapid incident response and proactive capacity planning.

items.text: How well does the organization define and manage service level agreements (SLAs) for IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: SLAs are not defined for IT services, or exist only in vendor contracts. Internal service performance expectations are informal and undocumented.
rubric.foundational: SLAs exist for some critical IT services but are not comprehensive. SLA metrics may be limited to availability with no coverage of performance, response time, or quality.
rubric.managed: SLAs are defined for all major IT services with measurable targets covering availability, performance, quality, and support responsiveness. SLA performance is monitored and reported regularly.
rubric.optimized: SLAs are dynamic, risk-based, and aligned with business impact. Experience-level agreements (XLAs) supplement traditional SLAs. Continuous monitoring with automated alerting ensures proactive SLA management.

items.text: To what extent does the organization link service costs to service levels, enabling value-based conversations?

rubric.ad_hoc: No connection exists between service costs and service levels. Requests for higher service levels are evaluated without understanding the cost implications.
rubric.foundational: Basic awareness exists that higher service levels cost more, but specific cost-to-service-level relationships are not quantified or documented.
rubric.managed: Service costs are linked to defined service tiers (e.g., Gold/Silver/Bronze). Business consumers can select service levels with clear cost implications, enabling informed trade-off decisions.
rubric.optimized: Granular cost-service level models enable precise value optimization. Business consumers actively manage service levels based on cost-benefit analysis. Service pricing is transparent and competitively benchmarked.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and report on customer satisfaction with IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: Customer satisfaction with IT services is not measured. Feedback is received only through informal complaints or escalations.
rubric.foundational: Periodic surveys or post-incident satisfaction ratings are collected, but response rates are low and results are not systematically analyzed or acted upon.
rubric.managed: Regular customer satisfaction measurement is performed through multiple channels (surveys, focus groups, service reviews). Results are analyzed by service, reported to leadership, and drive improvement plans.
rubric.optimized: Continuous experience monitoring combines survey data, behavioral analytics, and sentiment analysis. Customer satisfaction metrics are integrated into service management KPIs and influence investment priorities.

items.text: How well does the organization align IT service levels with federal mission requirements and citizen-facing service delivery standards?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT service levels are set based on technical capability rather than mission requirements. No connection exists between SLAs and the quality of citizen-facing service delivery.
rubric.foundational: Some high-visibility citizen-facing services have defined service levels, but alignment between IT SLAs and mission-specific requirements is inconsistent. Service level targets are primarily technology-focused.
rubric.managed: IT service levels are formally aligned with mission requirements and citizen-facing service standards. SLA targets for citizen-facing systems reflect 21st Century IDEA Act expectations and are reviewed with mission owners.
rubric.optimized: IT service levels are dynamically managed based on mission priority and citizen demand patterns. Real-time SLA monitoring is integrated with agency performance reporting. Service levels for citizen-facing services exceed industry benchmarks.

items.text: To what extent does the organization conduct regular service level reviews with business stakeholders and use results to drive service improvements?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service level reviews are not conducted. Business stakeholders are unaware of IT service performance targets or actual performance levels.
rubric.foundational: Annual or ad hoc service level reviews occur for some services, but participation from business stakeholders is limited. Review outcomes rarely drive concrete service improvement actions.
rubric.managed: Regular (monthly or quarterly) service level reviews are conducted with business stakeholders for all major services. Reviews include performance data, trend analysis, and agreed-upon improvement actions with timelines.
rubric.optimized: Continuous service level management is embedded in the operating model. Real-time performance dashboards are available to business stakeholders. Automated escalation processes trigger improvement actions when SLAs trend toward breach thresholds.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and report consumption of IT services by business unit, mission area, or customer?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT service consumption is not tracked by business consumer. The organization cannot determine which business units use which services or the relative volume of consumption.
rubric.foundational: Basic consumption data is available for some services (e.g., user counts, license assignments) but is not consolidated or reported systematically across the service portfolio.
rubric.managed: Consumption of all major IT services is tracked by business consumer with automated data collection. Monthly consumption reports are distributed to business stakeholders showing their usage patterns.
rubric.optimized: Real-time consumption dashboards provide granular visibility by service, consumer, and time period. Trend analysis and anomaly detection identify shifts in consumption patterns. Data drives demand management and capacity planning.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use service consumption data to inform showback or chargeback to business consumers?

rubric.ad_hoc: No showback or chargeback for IT services exists. Business units receive IT services without visibility into the costs they drive.
rubric.foundational: Basic showback reports are produced, showing business units their share of IT costs, but the methodology is allocation-based (e.g., headcount) rather than consumption-based.
rubric.managed: Consumption-based showback is implemented for most major services, providing business units with accurate visibility into the costs driven by their usage. Chargeback may be used for selected services.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive chargeback or detailed showback is implemented across all services using consumption-based metrics. Business consumers actively manage their IT consumption to optimize costs. Billing accuracy is validated and disputes are resolved systematically.

items.text: How well does the organization use service portfolio analytics to identify consolidation, rationalization, or optimization opportunities?

rubric.ad_hoc: No analytics are applied to the service portfolio. Redundant, underutilized, or end-of-life services are not systematically identified.
rubric.foundational: Occasional reviews identify obvious redundancies or underutilized services, but analysis is manual and not comprehensive. Action on findings is inconsistent.
rubric.managed: Regular portfolio analytics assess service utilization, redundancy, cost efficiency, and business alignment. Rationalization roadmaps are developed and executed based on findings.
rubric.optimized: Automated portfolio analytics continuously monitor the service landscape. Machine learning identifies consolidation opportunities and predicts service demand trends. Portfolio optimization is a continuous, data-driven practice.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and report IT service consumption patterns to enable demand forecasting and capacity planning?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT service consumption is not tracked at a granular level. Capacity planning is based on rough estimates and procurement cycles rather than actual consumption data.
rubric.foundational: Basic consumption metrics are collected for some services (e.g., storage usage, user counts), but data is not systematically analyzed for demand forecasting or capacity planning.
rubric.managed: Consumption data is collected for all major IT services and analyzed for trends, seasonality, and growth patterns. Consumption reports inform capacity planning decisions and budget forecasts.
rubric.optimized: Real-time consumption analytics drive automated capacity planning and demand forecasting. Predictive models anticipate consumption changes and trigger proactive scaling. Consumption data is integrated with financial models for cost projection.

items.text: How effectively does the organization calculate and communicate the unit cost of each IT service in the catalog?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT service costs are not calculated at the individual service level. Business consumers have no visibility into what specific services cost to deliver.
rubric.foundational: Unit costs are estimated for a few high-profile services, but calculations are rough and not based on comprehensive cost allocation. Cost information is not shared with consumers.
rubric.managed: Unit costs are calculated for all cataloged services using documented allocation methodologies. Costs are updated at least quarterly and communicated to business consumers through regular reports or dashboards.
rubric.optimized: Automated service cost models calculate real-time unit costs with full allocation of direct and indirect costs. Consumers can view per-unit costs and model the cost impact of consumption changes through self-service tools.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use service costing data to inform make-vs-buy and sourcing decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Sourcing decisions are made without understanding current in-house service costs. The organization cannot compare internal delivery costs to external alternatives.
rubric.foundational: In-house costs are roughly estimated for major sourcing decisions, but lack the rigor needed for valid comparison with external options. Hidden costs are frequently overlooked.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive service cost data supports rigorous make-vs-buy analysis for all significant sourcing decisions. Total cost comparisons include transition costs, risk factors, and ongoing management overhead.
rubric.optimized: Service costing is a foundational input to all sourcing decisions. Automated models compare in-house, outsourced, shared service, and cloud delivery options with full lifecycle cost analysis. Historical sourcing outcomes validate and improve cost models.

items.text: How well does the organization manage service pricing for showback or chargeback to consuming agencies or business units?

rubric.ad_hoc: No service pricing model exists. Consuming agencies or business units do not receive information about the cost of IT services they consume.
rubric.foundational: High-level cost allocations are communicated to consumers (e.g., total IT cost per business unit), but pricing is not at the service level and does not reflect actual consumption patterns.
rubric.managed: Service-level pricing is established for major IT services with documented pricing methodologies. Consumers receive regular statements showing services consumed and associated costs.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic, consumption-based pricing is in place for all cataloged services. Pricing models include tiered rates, volume discounts, and transparent cost components. Consumers can model costs before making consumption decisions.

items.text: How well does the organization use service cost data to inform make-vs-buy decisions and service sourcing strategies?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service cost data is not used in sourcing decisions. Make-vs-buy choices are based on incumbent vendor relationships, procurement convenience, or technical preference rather than cost analysis.
rubric.foundational: Cost data is referenced in major sourcing decisions, but analysis is limited to direct costs without considering total cost of ownership, transition costs, or strategic value.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive service cost analysis informs all significant make-vs-buy decisions. Analysis includes TCO, transition costs, risk factors, and strategic alignment. Results are documented in business cases per OMB Circular A-76 guidance where applicable.
rubric.optimized: Service cost data drives continuous sourcing optimization. The organization maintains a sourcing strategy for each service category based on cost-effectiveness, market analysis, and mission criticality. Regular market testing validates sourcing decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage digital services as part of the IT service portfolio aligned with 21st Century IDEA Act requirements?

rubric.ad_hoc: Digital services are not managed as part of the service portfolio. Public-facing digital services are developed and operated independently without portfolio-level governance.
rubric.foundational: Digital services are recognized as part of the portfolio but lack standardized management practices. Compliance with 21st Century IDEA Act is addressed reactively.
rubric.managed: Digital services are fully integrated into the service portfolio with consistent governance, lifecycle management, and cost tracking. Customer experience metrics are tracked alongside traditional IT service metrics.
rubric.optimized: The organization is a leader in digital service management, with integrated portfolio governance covering all digital touchpoints. Continuous user research, analytics, and A/B testing drive service improvement. Full compliance with 21st Century IDEA Act is demonstrated through measurable outcomes.

items.text: To what extent does the organization measure and optimize the cost-effectiveness of citizen-facing digital services?

rubric.ad_hoc: The cost of delivering citizen-facing digital services is unknown. No metrics link digital service spending to citizen outcomes or transaction volumes.
rubric.foundational: Basic cost tracking exists for digital service platforms, but cost-per-transaction or cost-per-user metrics are not calculated. Digital service spending is not optimized.
rubric.managed: Cost-per-transaction and cost-per-user metrics are calculated for major citizen-facing services. Cost trends are analyzed, and optimization opportunities (e.g., self-service adoption, channel shift) are pursued.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive digital service economics are understood, including cost per transaction by channel, automation rates, and citizen satisfaction per dollar spent. Analytics drive continuous optimization of the digital service portfolio.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the portfolio of APIs and platform services that enable internal and external digital capabilities?

rubric.ad_hoc: APIs and platform services are not managed as a portfolio. Integration capabilities are built ad hoc, leading to duplication and inconsistent interfaces.
rubric.foundational: A partial inventory of APIs exists, but platform services are not managed holistically. No cost accounting for API/platform services is performed.
rubric.managed: APIs and platform services are cataloged, governed, and costed as part of the service portfolio. Usage metrics, consumer counts, and cost allocations are tracked for each platform capability.
rubric.optimized: A mature API/platform strategy treats reusable capabilities as products with clear ownership, SLAs, cost models, and roadmaps. Analytics drive investment in high-value platform services and retirement of underutilized capabilities.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and report the business value and citizen experience impact of its digital services?

rubric.ad_hoc: Digital service value is not measured beyond basic availability and uptime metrics. No connection is made between digital service delivery and mission outcomes or citizen satisfaction.
rubric.foundational: Some digital services track usage metrics (e.g., page views, transaction counts), but business value and citizen experience are not systematically measured or reported.
rubric.managed: Digital services are evaluated using business value metrics (e.g., cost avoidance, processing time reduction) and citizen experience measures (e.g., customer satisfaction scores, task completion rates) aligned with 21st Century IDEA Act requirements.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive digital service value measurement is continuous and automated. Real-time citizen experience analytics drive service improvements. Value metrics are integrated into agency performance reporting and inform digital investment prioritization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and rationalize duplicative or overlapping IT services across the enterprise?

rubric.ad_hoc: No systematic effort exists to identify duplicative IT services. Multiple bureaus or programs may operate similar services independently without awareness of overlap.
rubric.foundational: Ad hoc reviews occasionally identify duplicative services, but no systematic process evaluates the portfolio for overlap. Consolidation decisions are driven by budget pressure rather than strategic analysis.
rubric.managed: Regular portfolio rationalization reviews use service mapping and capability analysis to identify overlapping services. Consolidation roadmaps are developed with cost-benefit analysis and stakeholder engagement.
rubric.optimized: Continuous portfolio rationalization uses automated service mapping, usage analytics, and cost analysis to identify rationalization opportunities. A governed process evaluates and executes consolidation with measured outcomes. Portfolio complexity metrics show declining trends.

items.text: To what extent does the organization evaluate service fitness (cost, quality, relevance) to determine which services to invest in, sustain, or retire?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service fitness is not evaluated. All services receive sustained funding regardless of utilization, cost-effectiveness, or alignment with current mission needs.
rubric.foundational: Service fitness is informally assessed for the most visible services, but no structured evaluation methodology exists. Retirement decisions are rare and politically driven.
rubric.managed: A structured service fitness evaluation assesses cost-effectiveness, utilization, business alignment, and technical health for all services on a regular cycle. Results drive invest/sustain/retire decisions.
rubric.optimized: Automated service fitness scoring continuously evaluates the portfolio across multiple dimensions. Portfolio governance acts on fitness data to optimize investment allocation. The organization demonstrates measurable improvement in portfolio health metrics over time.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the retirement and decommission of IT services to free up budget for higher-value investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT services are rarely retired. Legacy services continue to consume budget and resources indefinitely because no decommission process exists.
rubric.foundational: Service retirement occurs occasionally but is ad hoc and often incomplete. Decommission costs and timelines are not planned, and resources freed by retirement are not systematically redirected.
rubric.managed: A formal service retirement process includes user migration planning, data archival, decommission execution, and cost recapture. Freed resources are tracked and redirected to approved priorities.
rubric.optimized: Service retirement is a well-practiced capability with standardized processes, automated checklists, and proven playbooks. The organization maintains a retirement pipeline and regularly demonstrates budget reallocation from retired services to modernization initiatives.

items.text: How systematically does the organization evaluate and retire redundant, obsolete, or underperforming IT services from its portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: No systematic service rationalization occurs. Redundant services persist indefinitely, and retirement decisions are made only when forced by vendor end-of-life or system failure.
rubric.foundational: Ad hoc rationalization efforts occur periodically, typically driven by budget pressure or audit findings. No formal criteria or process exists for evaluating services for retirement.
rubric.managed: A formal service rationalization program evaluates all services against defined criteria (utilization, cost, strategic alignment, technical health). Rationalization reviews occur annually with documented decisions and retirement plans.
rubric.optimized: Continuous service rationalization is embedded in portfolio management. Automated analysis identifies rationalization candidates based on real-time utilization, cost, and performance data. A managed retirement pipeline ensures orderly service transitions and cost savings realization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization map and manage dependencies between IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service dependencies are not documented. Changes to one service may unexpectedly impact dependent services because relationships are not understood.
rubric.foundational: Dependencies are known informally for major services, but no comprehensive dependency map exists. Impact analysis for changes relies on tribal knowledge.
rubric.managed: Service dependency mapping documents relationships between all significant IT services. Dependency data supports impact analysis for changes, incidents, and retirement decisions. Maps are maintained in a CMDB or equivalent.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic service dependency management uses automated discovery and real-time monitoring to maintain accurate dependency maps. Impact analysis is automated. Dependency data drives architecture decisions, change management, and resilience planning.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage multi-vendor service integration across the IT service portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: Service integration across vendors is ad hoc. No defined model governs how services from different vendors work together, leading to finger-pointing during incidents and gaps in accountability.
rubric.foundational: Basic integration points between vendor services are defined, but a comprehensive service integration model is lacking. Incident resolution across vendor boundaries is slow and contentious.
rubric.managed: A service integration and management (SIAM) model governs multi-vendor service delivery. Clear accountability, integration standards, and cross-vendor processes ensure seamless service delivery. An integration layer orchestrates vendor interactions.
rubric.optimized: Mature multi-vendor service integration provides a unified service experience regardless of delivery source. Automated integration, cross-vendor performance management, and continuous optimization ensure cost-effective, high-quality service delivery across the vendor ecosystem.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the IT service portfolio to support hybrid work and distributed workforce needs?

rubric.ad_hoc: The service portfolio was designed for office-based work and has not been adapted for hybrid or distributed workforce needs. Remote workers have degraded service experiences.
rubric.foundational: Some services have been adapted for remote access, but the portfolio does not holistically address hybrid work needs. Service levels may differ significantly between office and remote workers.
rubric.managed: The service portfolio has been redesigned to deliver consistent, high-quality services regardless of work location. Service level commitments apply equally to remote and office workers. Collaboration and productivity services are optimized for hybrid work.
rubric.optimized: The service portfolio is fully optimized for hybrid and distributed work, with location-independent service delivery, adaptive quality of service, and continuous user experience monitoring. Portfolio decisions proactively address evolving workforce models and emerging collaboration patterns.

items.text: How mature is the organization's process for receiving, documenting, and managing IT demand from business stakeholders?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT demand is communicated informally through emails, hallway conversations, or direct requests to individual IT staff. No central intake process exists, leading to lost requests and duplicated efforts.
rubric.foundational: A basic demand intake process exists (e.g., request forms, ticketing system) but is not consistently used across the organization. Demand backlog is not centrally managed or prioritized.
rubric.managed: A standardized demand intake process captures all IT requests through a central system. Each request is documented with business justification, priority, estimated effort, and alignment to strategic objectives.
rubric.optimized: Demand intake is fully automated with self-service capabilities, AI-assisted categorization, and automatic routing. The demand pipeline provides real-time visibility into all requests, their status, and capacity implications.

items.text: How effectively does the organization evaluate and prioritize IT demand against strategic objectives and available capacity?

rubric.ad_hoc: Prioritization is based on the loudest voice or most senior requester. No formal criteria exist for evaluating demand against strategy or capacity constraints.
rubric.foundational: Basic prioritization criteria exist (e.g., urgency, executive sponsorship) but are applied inconsistently. Capacity constraints are not formally considered in prioritization decisions.
rubric.managed: A formal prioritization framework evaluates demand against strategic alignment, business value, risk, cost, and capacity. A governance body reviews and approves demand on a regular cadence.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic prioritization uses quantitative scoring models, real-time capacity data, and portfolio impact analysis. Scenario modeling evaluates trade-offs between competing demands. Prioritization is transparent and auditable.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage demand across the full lifecycle, from intake through delivery and value realization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Demand is managed only through initial intake and approval. Once approved, requests enter project or operational queues with no visibility into progress or outcome tracking.
rubric.foundational: Approved demand is tracked through delivery, but the connection between the original demand, the delivered solution, and the realized business value is not maintained.
rubric.managed: Demand is managed end-to-end with visibility from intake through delivery and post-implementation review. Value realization is assessed for significant demand items.
rubric.optimized: Full lifecycle demand management integrates intake, prioritization, delivery tracking, and value realization into a single view. Feedback loops ensure lessons from delivered demand inform future intake and prioritization.

items.text: How well does the organization classify and route IT demand requests to ensure appropriate governance and fulfillment pathways?

rubric.ad_hoc: All IT demands enter through the same channel regardless of size, type, or complexity. No classification system routes requests to appropriate decision-making or fulfillment processes.
rubric.foundational: Basic classification distinguishes between service requests and project requests, but routing is inconsistent. Small requests may require the same approval process as major investments.
rubric.managed: A demand classification framework categorizes requests by type (service request, enhancement, project, strategic initiative), size, and complexity. Each category has an appropriate governance and fulfillment pathway with defined cycle time targets.
rubric.optimized: Intelligent demand classification uses automated categorization, dynamic routing, and right-sized governance. Machine learning improves classification accuracy over time. Express lanes for small requests coexist with rigorous governance for major investments. Classification data informs portfolio analytics.

items.text: How well does the organization prioritize and triage incoming IT demand requests against available capacity, strategic alignment, and mission urgency?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal prioritization or triage of IT demands exists. Requests are handled first-come-first-served or based on the political influence of the requesting organization.
rubric.foundational: Basic prioritization occurs for large requests, but criteria are informal and inconsistently applied. Small and medium requests bypass any prioritization process entirely.
rubric.managed: A formal triage process evaluates all IT demand requests against defined criteria including strategic alignment, mission urgency, resource availability, and cost-benefit. Prioritized demand queues are visible to all stakeholders.
rubric.optimized: Demand triage is automated with scoring algorithms that weigh strategic alignment, mission criticality, cost-benefit, and capacity constraints. Real-time demand dashboards show pipeline status, and dynamic reprioritization occurs as conditions change.

items.text: How effectively does the organization plan IT capacity (staff, infrastructure, budget) to meet anticipated demand?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal capacity planning exists. IT resources are allocated reactively as demand materializes, leading to bottlenecks, delays, and fire drills.
rubric.foundational: Basic capacity planning is performed annually during budget season, but plans are not updated as demand changes. Capacity shortfalls are discovered when delivery timelines slip.
rubric.managed: Capacity planning is performed quarterly or more frequently, incorporating demand forecasts, resource utilization data, and planned initiatives. Capacity gaps are identified early and mitigation plans developed.
rubric.optimized: Continuous capacity planning uses real-time utilization data, predictive demand models, and automated scenario analysis. Capacity is dynamically adjusted through flexible resourcing models (e.g., flex staffing, elastic cloud).

items.text: How well does the organization shape demand to optimize IT resource utilization and cost efficiency?

rubric.ad_hoc: No demand shaping occurs. All demand is accepted or rejected without considering alternatives, timing adjustments, or standardized solutions that could reduce cost or effort.
rubric.foundational: Informal demand shaping occurs (e.g., suggesting existing solutions instead of custom development) but is not a systematic practice.
rubric.managed: Formal demand shaping practices include promoting standard solutions, timing demand to optimize resource utilization, and offering tiered service options to manage cost expectations.
rubric.optimized: Advanced demand shaping uses consumption analytics, dynamic pricing (showback/chargeback), and proactive engagement with business partners to optimize the demand profile. Business stakeholders actively manage their demand in response to cost signals.

items.text: To what extent does the organization forecast future IT demand using data-driven methods?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT demand is not forecasted. The organization is constantly surprised by new requests and cannot anticipate resource needs.
rubric.foundational: Basic demand forecasting uses historical request volumes and known upcoming projects. Forecasts are produced annually and not updated based on changing conditions.
rubric.managed: Demand forecasting incorporates historical trends, business growth plans, technology roadmaps, and stakeholder input. Forecasts are updated quarterly and reviewed with leadership.
rubric.optimized: Predictive analytics and machine learning forecast demand based on multiple data sources including business metrics, market conditions, and technology trends. Forecasts are continuously refined and highly accurate.

items.text: How effectively does the organization balance IT capacity across competing demands from multiple mission areas and business units?

rubric.ad_hoc: Capacity allocation across mission areas is not managed. Resource conflicts are resolved reactively through escalation to senior leadership, causing delays and mission impact.
rubric.foundational: Basic capacity allocation considers major mission priorities, but granular balancing across business units is not performed. Resource contention is a recurring issue during peak demand periods.
rubric.managed: Capacity is formally allocated across mission areas using defined priorities and governance processes. Resource contention is managed proactively through capacity planning and demand shaping techniques.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic capacity management balances resources across mission areas in near-real-time. Elastic cloud resources supplement fixed capacity during demand spikes. Capacity allocation decisions are data-driven and aligned with agency priority framework.

items.text: How mature is the organization's ability to show business consumers the IT costs driven by their consumption?

rubric.ad_hoc: Business consumers have no visibility into the IT costs associated with their consumption. IT costs are treated as overhead with no attribution to business units.
rubric.foundational: High-level cost allocation reports show business units their share of IT spending, but allocations are based on proxies (e.g., headcount, revenue) rather than actual consumption.
rubric.managed: Showback reports provide business consumers with consumption-based cost visibility for most IT services. Reports are produced monthly and include enough detail for consumers to understand their cost drivers.
rubric.optimized: Real-time showback dashboards provide granular cost visibility by service, resource type, and time period. Business consumers can drill into costs, compare to budgets, and identify optimization opportunities independently.

items.text: To what extent does showback/chargeback influence business consumer behavior regarding IT demand?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cost attribution does not exist, so business consumers have no financial incentive to manage IT consumption or make cost-conscious decisions.
rubric.foundational: Showback reports are distributed but rarely influence behavior. Business consumers view IT costs as fixed overhead rather than a variable they can manage.
rubric.managed: Showback/chargeback information actively influences business consumer behavior. Consumers consider cost implications when making demand decisions and engage with IT to identify optimization opportunities.
rubric.optimized: Cost transparency drives a culture of cost-conscious IT consumption. Business consumers proactively manage their IT spending, trade off between service options based on cost, and collaborate with IT on efficiency improvements.

items.text: How well does the organization handle the governance and dispute resolution aspects of showback/chargeback?

rubric.ad_hoc: No governance exists for cost attribution. When allocations are shared, business units frequently challenge the accuracy and fairness of allocations with no resolution process.
rubric.foundational: Basic governance exists (e.g., defined allocation methodology) but dispute resolution is informal. Allocation methodology changes are made without stakeholder input.
rubric.managed: A formal governance process oversees showback/chargeback including methodology approval, regular reviews, and a defined dispute resolution process. Stakeholders have input into allocation methodology changes.
rubric.optimized: Governance is transparent and collaborative, with business stakeholders actively participating in methodology reviews. Disputes are rare due to high data accuracy and consumption-based attribution. Continuous improvement is driven by stakeholder feedback.

items.text: How well does the organization use showback or chargeback data to influence business unit behavior and optimize IT consumption patterns?

rubric.ad_hoc: No showback or chargeback mechanism exists. Business units consume IT resources without visibility into costs, leading to unconstrained demand growth.
rubric.foundational: Basic showback reports are provided to business units, but the data is not granular enough to influence behavior. Business units view IT costs as overhead rather than a consumable resource.
rubric.managed: Showback data is granular, timely, and presented in business terms. Business units understand their IT consumption costs and consider cost implications when making demand decisions. Some cost-conscious behavior changes are evident.
rubric.optimized: Showback/chargeback is a powerful demand management lever. Business units actively manage their IT consumption, negotiate service levels based on cost trade-offs, and fund new capabilities by optimizing existing usage. Consumption-based pricing drives efficient resource utilization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization forecast IT demand across infrastructure, applications, and services?

rubric.ad_hoc: No IT demand forecasting exists. Capacity is added reactively in response to performance issues or user complaints, often resulting in emergency procurements.
rubric.foundational: Basic demand forecasting exists for a few areas (e.g., storage growth, email user counts), but forecasts are based on simple trend extrapolation without business input.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive demand forecasting covers all major IT services and infrastructure categories. Forecasts incorporate business growth plans, seasonal patterns, and planned initiatives. Accuracy is tracked and improved over time.
rubric.optimized: Advanced demand forecasting uses predictive analytics, machine learning, and business intelligence to anticipate needs before they arise. Automated models adjust forecasts based on real-time consumption signals and planned business changes.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use demand data to inform IT investment and capacity decisions proactively?

rubric.ad_hoc: Demand data is not used for investment or capacity planning. Decisions are reactive, driven by outages, slow performance, or user frustration.
rubric.foundational: Demand data is collected but not systematically used for planning. Investment requests cite demand in qualitative terms rather than quantitative forecasts.
rubric.managed: Demand analytics directly inform infrastructure capacity planning, application scaling decisions, and investment prioritization. Quantitative demand forecasts are a required input to business cases.
rubric.optimized: Demand intelligence is a strategic capability, enabling the organization to anticipate and pre-position capacity, optimize procurement timing, and align investments with emerging mission needs. Demand data is integrated with financial planning and scenario modeling.

items.text: How well does the organization manage and influence IT demand to optimize resource utilization and spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: No demand management or shaping occurs. Users consume IT resources without awareness of cost implications, and the organization makes no effort to influence consumption patterns.
rubric.foundational: Basic demand management exists through rationing or approval queues, but the organization does not actively shape demand through pricing signals, self-service options, or policy guidance.
rubric.managed: Active demand management uses showback data, consumption dashboards, and policy guidance to influence user behavior. Standard service tiers and self-service provisioning help channel demand toward cost-effective options.
rubric.optimized: Sophisticated demand management combines economic signals (chargeback, tiered pricing), behavioral nudges (consumption dashboards, recommendations), and policy levers to optimize demand. Demand shaping strategies are measured for effectiveness and continuously refined.

items.text: How well does the organization forecast IT demand growth driven by new legislative mandates, policy changes, or mission expansions?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT demand impacts from legislative or policy changes are not forecasted. New mandates result in emergency resource requests and reactive capacity scrambles.
rubric.foundational: Ad hoc assessments of IT demand impact are performed when major mandates are known, but no systematic process links legislative tracking to IT demand forecasting.
rubric.managed: IT demand forecasting formally incorporates legislative tracking, policy analysis, and mission expansion plans. Impact assessments estimate resource, timeline, and budget implications of anticipated changes.
rubric.optimized: Predictive demand models integrate real-time legislative and policy tracking, interagency coordination intelligence, and mission growth projections. Scenario-based forecasts enable proactive resource positioning and are integrated with the agency's Congressional affairs and strategic planning functions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and manage shadow IT spending and services?

rubric.ad_hoc: Shadow IT is not monitored or managed. Unknown applications, cloud services, and IT spending exist outside IT governance with no visibility or risk assessment.
rubric.foundational: The organization is aware that shadow IT exists and has conducted ad hoc discovery efforts, but no ongoing process identifies or governs shadow IT spending.
rubric.managed: Systematic shadow IT discovery processes (e.g., cloud access security brokers, network analysis, expense report mining) identify unauthorized IT spending. A governance framework addresses discovered shadow IT through a risk-based approach.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive shadow IT management combines automated discovery, risk assessment, and governance. Discovered services are evaluated for enterprise adoption or phase-out. Root cause analysis of shadow IT demand identifies gaps in the official service catalog.

items.text: To what extent does the organization understand and address the root causes of shadow IT demand?

rubric.ad_hoc: Root causes of shadow IT are not investigated. Shadow IT is viewed as a compliance problem rather than a signal of unmet business needs.
rubric.foundational: Anecdotal understanding of shadow IT drivers exists (e.g., slow procurement, limited catalog options), but no systematic analysis connects shadow IT demand to gaps in official IT offerings.
rubric.managed: Root cause analysis of shadow IT identifies specific gaps in the IT service catalog, procurement process, or user experience that drive users to seek alternatives. Findings inform service improvement plans.
rubric.optimized: Continuous feedback loops connect shadow IT discovery to service portfolio improvement. The organization proactively evolves its service catalog to address emerging needs before shadow IT takes root. Shadow IT metrics are declining trend-over-trend.

items.text: How well does the organization quantify the total cost and risk of shadow IT across the enterprise?

rubric.ad_hoc: The cost and risk of shadow IT are unknown. No attempt has been made to quantify unauthorized IT spending or assess associated security and compliance risks.
rubric.foundational: Rough estimates of shadow IT spending exist based on limited discovery efforts. Risk assessments are qualitative and cover only known instances of unauthorized IT services.
rubric.managed: Shadow IT spending is quantified through systematic discovery and expense analysis. Risk assessments evaluate security, compliance, data management, and vendor risks for identified shadow IT services.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive shadow IT economics are understood, including direct costs, hidden costs (support, integration, data management), and risk exposure. Shadow IT metrics are reported to leadership and drive governance improvement. Trends show measurable reduction in unauthorized spending.

items.text: How effectively does the organization provide self-service IT provisioning capabilities to reduce demand on central IT teams?

rubric.ad_hoc: No self-service IT capabilities exist. All IT requests require manual processing by IT staff, creating bottlenecks and long fulfillment times.
rubric.foundational: Limited self-service exists for basic requests (e.g., password resets, software catalog), but most IT provisioning requires manual IT involvement. Self-service adoption is low.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive self-service portals enable users to provision approved services, manage access, and resolve common issues without IT intervention. Self-service usage is tracked and expanding.
rubric.optimized: Advanced self-service leverages automation, AI-assisted support, and pre-approved service templates. Users can provision infrastructure, applications, and workspace resources with automated governance controls. Self-service handles the majority of routine IT requests.

items.text: To what extent does the organization automate IT service fulfillment to improve speed and reduce per-transaction costs?
