rubric.ad_hoc: IT service fulfillment is manual. Each request requires human processing, resulting in high per-transaction costs, inconsistent quality, and long fulfillment times.
rubric.foundational: Some common fulfillment workflows are partially automated (e.g., account provisioning, endpoint deployment), but most processes require manual steps. Automation is not strategically planned.
rubric.managed: Service fulfillment automation covers most high-volume, repeatable processes. Automated workflows integrate across ITSM, identity management, and provisioning systems. Per-transaction costs and fulfillment times are tracked and declining.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive fulfillment automation uses orchestration platforms to automate end-to-end workflows across all IT domains. Zero-touch provisioning handles routine requests. Cost per transaction is benchmarked and continuously optimized. Automation frees IT staff for higher-value work.

items.text: How well does the organization use demand analytics to identify patterns, predict future needs, and optimize service delivery?

rubric.ad_hoc: No demand analytics capability exists. Service request patterns are not analyzed, and demand planning relies on anecdotal observations.
rubric.foundational: Basic demand reports show request volumes and trends, but analysis is retrospective and does not inform proactive planning or resource allocation.
rubric.managed: Demand analytics identify patterns (seasonal spikes, emerging needs, underserved populations) and inform proactive capacity planning and service catalog evolution. Predictive models anticipate demand changes.
rubric.optimized: Advanced demand analytics use machine learning to predict demand, identify emerging needs, and optimize resource allocation in real time. Insights drive proactive service development, staffing decisions, and self-service improvements.

items.text: How effectively does the organization govern and prioritize competing IT demands across business units and mission areas?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal demand governance exists. Competing IT demands are resolved through escalation, political influence, or first-come-first-served allocation.
rubric.foundational: Basic demand governance exists (e.g., a project review board), but prioritization criteria are vague, inconsistently applied, or dominated by the loudest voices.
rubric.managed: A formal demand governance process uses defined criteria (strategic alignment, mission impact, cost, risk) to prioritize competing demands. Cross-functional participation ensures balanced decision-making.
rubric.optimized: Mature demand governance integrates with portfolio management, capacity planning, and financial management. Dynamic prioritization responds to changing mission needs. Transparent processes build stakeholder confidence in allocation fairness.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage IT demand at the enterprise level versus allowing fragmented, bureau-level demand management?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT demand is managed independently by each bureau or program with no enterprise coordination. Duplicative requests, conflicting priorities, and missed consolidation opportunities are common.
rubric.foundational: Enterprise-level demand management exists for some categories (e.g., major investments), but most demand is managed at the bureau level. Enterprise visibility into aggregate demand is limited.
rubric.managed: Enterprise demand management provides visibility into all significant IT demands across bureaus. Consolidation opportunities are identified, and enterprise priorities guide allocation. Bureaus retain some autonomy within enterprise governance guardrails.
rubric.optimized: Integrated enterprise demand management balances central governance with bureau autonomy. Enterprise analytics identify cross-cutting needs and consolidation opportunities. Demand data flows into enterprise portfolio, capacity, and budget planning.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the relationship between IT demand, capacity, and cost to make informed trade-off decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Demand, capacity, and cost are managed independently. The organization cannot model how changes in demand affect capacity needs and costs, leading to misalignment and waste.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that demand, capacity, and cost are related, but integrated analysis connecting these dimensions is not performed. Trade-off decisions are made without complete information.
rubric.managed: Integrated demand-capacity-cost analysis enables informed trade-off decisions. When demand exceeds capacity, leaders can evaluate options (add capacity, defer demand, reduce service levels) with clear cost implications.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic demand-capacity-cost modeling provides real-time decision support. Leaders can interactively explore trade-offs and scenario outcomes. Optimization algorithms suggest the most cost-effective ways to meet demand while maintaining required service levels.

items.text: How effectively does the organization align IT demand management with agency strategic planning and mission priorities?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT demand management and agency strategic planning are disconnected. IT receives demand requests without understanding how they connect to strategic priorities or mission outcomes.
rubric.foundational: IT demand is loosely connected to strategic plans through annual planning exercises, but ongoing demand management does not reference strategic priorities when making intake or prioritization decisions.
rubric.managed: IT demand management is integrated with agency strategic planning. Demand intake criteria include strategic alignment scoring. Resource allocation decisions explicitly reference mission priorities and strategic goals.
rubric.optimized: Seamless alignment between strategic planning and demand management ensures IT resources are continuously directed toward the highest-value mission needs. Dynamic demand prioritization responds to evolving strategic priorities. IT is a proactive partner in shaping mission-driven demand.

items.text: To what extent do business stakeholders have visibility into the IT demand pipeline and understand how their requests are prioritized?

rubric.ad_hoc: Business stakeholders submit IT requests with no visibility into the demand pipeline. They have no understanding of how or when their requests will be evaluated or fulfilled.
rubric.foundational: Basic status tracking allows requestors to check whether their requests are received, but pipeline visibility is limited. Prioritization criteria are not shared with business stakeholders.
rubric.managed: Business stakeholders have visibility into the demand pipeline through dashboards or portals showing request status, queue position, and prioritization rationale. Regular communication updates stakeholders on pipeline decisions.
rubric.optimized: Full demand pipeline transparency provides business stakeholders with real-time visibility, interactive priority modeling, and collaborative planning tools. Stakeholders understand trade-offs and can participate in prioritization discussions with complete information.

items.text: How effectively does the organization capture and analyze unmet IT demand to inform strategic planning and investment decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Unmet IT demand is not tracked. Rejected or deferred requests disappear from view, and the organization has no visibility into the gap between business needs and IT delivery capacity.
rubric.foundational: Some record of deferred or rejected requests exists, but data is not analyzed systematically. Patterns of unmet demand are not identified or communicated to leadership.
rubric.managed: Unmet demand is systematically tracked, categorized, and analyzed. Reports identify patterns of unmet demand by business area, capability type, and strategic theme. Analysis informs annual IT strategic planning and budget formulation.
rubric.optimized: Unmet demand analytics are integrated into strategic planning and investment prioritization. Predictive models forecast future demand gaps, enabling proactive capacity investment. Unmet demand data drives business cases for new capabilities and informs OMB budget submissions.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT investment governance framework?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal IT investment governance exists. Investment decisions are made ad hoc by individual leaders without consistent criteria, portfolio visibility, or cross-organizational coordination.
rubric.foundational: Basic governance exists (e.g., an IT investment review board) but meets infrequently, reviews only large projects, and uses inconsistent evaluation criteria.
rubric.managed: A formal IT investment governance framework operates with defined roles, regular meeting cadence, standardized evaluation criteria, and portfolio-level visibility. All investments above a defined threshold require governance approval.
rubric.optimized: Investment governance is integrated with enterprise strategic planning and TBM data. Real-time portfolio dashboards inform governance decisions. Delegated authorities and tiered review processes optimize decision speed without sacrificing rigor.

items.text: How effectively does the organization balance its IT investment portfolio across run, grow, and transform categories?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization does not categorize investments by run/grow/transform (or equivalent). Investment decisions are made individually without portfolio-level perspective on the balance of spending.
rubric.foundational: Investments are loosely categorized as run, grow, or transform, but categorization is inconsistent and the portfolio balance is not actively managed.
rubric.managed: The investment portfolio is categorized and tracked by run/grow/transform with target allocations. Actual vs. target balance is reported to leadership and informs funding decisions.
rubric.optimized: Portfolio balance is actively managed with dynamic rebalancing based on strategic priorities, market conditions, and business needs. Scenario modeling evaluates the impact of portfolio shifts on business outcomes.

items.text: To what extent does the organization apply consistent, transparent criteria for evaluating and comparing IT investment proposals?

rubric.ad_hoc: Investment proposals are evaluated based on subjective judgment or individual sponsor advocacy. No standard evaluation criteria exist, making it impossible to compare proposals objectively.
rubric.foundational: Basic evaluation criteria exist (e.g., cost, strategic alignment) but are applied inconsistently. The criteria may be qualitative and difficult to use for objective comparison.
rubric.managed: Standardized quantitative and qualitative criteria are applied to all investment proposals, including business value, strategic alignment, risk, cost, and feasibility. Proposals are scored and ranked systematically.
rubric.optimized: Multi-criteria decision analysis combines quantitative modeling with strategic judgment. Criteria weights reflect current priorities and are adjusted as strategy evolves. Portfolio optimization algorithms maximize value within budget constraints.

items.text: How effectively does the organization ensure CIO authority over IT investments as required by FITARA and Clinger-Cohen Act?

rubric.ad_hoc: CIO authority over IT investments is limited. Bureaus and programs procure IT independently, and the CIO lacks visibility or approval authority over a significant portion of IT spending.
rubric.foundational: CIO review authority is established in policy, but enforcement is inconsistent. Some IT investments bypass CIO review through classification as non-IT or through delegated procurement authority.
rubric.managed: CIO authority is consistently exercised over all IT investments per FITARA requirements. A defined review process ensures CIO visibility into and approval authority over IT spending across the enterprise.
rubric.optimized: CIO authority is a strategic enabler that drives portfolio optimization, prevents duplication, and ensures alignment with enterprise architecture and IT strategy. The CIO office provides value-added review that improves investment quality rather than simply adding process overhead.

items.text: How effectively does the CIO exercise authority over IT investment decisions as mandated by FITARA, including approval, disapproval, and de-obligation of IT spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: The CIO has limited visibility into IT investment decisions across the agency. Significant IT spending occurs outside CIO authority, and FITARA Section 831 requirements for CIO budget authority are not fully implemented.
rubric.foundational: The CIO reviews major IT investments but does not have effective authority to disapprove or redirect spending. FITARA authority is acknowledged in policy but not consistently exercised in practice.
rubric.managed: The CIO exercises clear authority over IT investment decisions, including review and approval of all IT acquisitions above defined thresholds. FITARA Section 831 requirements are fully implemented with documented delegation and escalation processes.
rubric.optimized: CIO IT investment authority is fully operationalized with real-time visibility into all IT spending decisions. The CIO proactively shapes the investment portfolio through strategic guidance, and FITARA authority extends to effective oversight of bureau/component IT spending.

items.text: How rigorous are the business cases developed for IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Business cases are not required for IT investments, or are perfunctory one-page documents with vague benefit claims and no financial analysis.
rubric.foundational: Business cases are required for major investments and include cost estimates and high-level benefit descriptions, but financial analysis is limited and assumptions are not well-documented.
rubric.managed: Business cases follow a standardized template with detailed cost analysis, quantified benefits, risk assessment, alternatives considered, and implementation timeline. Assumptions are documented and sensitivity analysis is performed.
rubric.optimized: Business cases are data-driven, incorporating TBM cost data, benchmarks, and validated benefit models. Independent review ensures objectivity. Business cases are living documents updated through implementation to track actual vs. projected outcomes.

items.text: How effectively does the organization quantify and validate the expected benefits of IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Benefits are described in vague qualitative terms (e.g., 'improved efficiency,' 'better service') without quantification. No validation of claimed benefits occurs.
rubric.foundational: Benefits are estimated quantitatively for major investments, but estimates are based on assumptions rather than data. Post-implementation benefit validation is not performed.
rubric.managed: Benefits are quantified using documented methodologies with clear baselines, metrics, and measurement plans. Post-implementation reviews validate realized benefits for significant investments.
rubric.optimized: A benefits realization management program tracks benefits from business case through delivery and into operations. Historical benefit realization data improves future estimation accuracy. Unrealized benefits trigger corrective action.

items.text: To what extent does the organization assess and manage risk as part of the IT investment decision process?

rubric.ad_hoc: Risk is not formally assessed as part of investment decisions. Investments are approved based on anticipated benefits without systematic consideration of what could go wrong.
rubric.foundational: Basic risk identification is performed for major investments, but risk assessment is qualitative and risk mitigation strategies are generic.
rubric.managed: A structured risk assessment framework is applied to all significant investments, evaluating technical, organizational, financial, and schedule risks. Risk mitigation plans are required and tracked.
rubric.optimized: Quantitative risk analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation) informs investment decisions. Portfolio-level risk is monitored and managed. Risk appetite statements guide investment selection, and risk outcomes inform future assessments.

items.text: How well does the organization validate investment business case assumptions through post-implementation reviews and benefits realization tracking?

rubric.ad_hoc: Post-implementation reviews are not conducted. Once an investment is approved and deployed, business case assumptions are never validated against actual outcomes.
rubric.foundational: Post-implementation reviews are performed for a few high-visibility investments, but the practice is not systematic. Benefits realization is tracked informally if at all.
rubric.managed: Post-implementation reviews are conducted for all major investments at defined intervals. Benefits realization is formally tracked against business case projections, and lessons learned inform future business case development.
rubric.optimized: Continuous benefits realization management is embedded in investment governance. Automated tracking compares actual outcomes to projected benefits in real time. Variance analysis feeds back into business case models and improves estimation accuracy across the portfolio.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track the realized value of IT investments after implementation?

rubric.ad_hoc: No post-implementation value tracking occurs. Once an investment is funded and delivered, outcomes are not measured against the original business case.
rubric.foundational: Post-implementation reviews are conducted for some major investments, but reviews focus on delivery (on-time, on-budget) rather than value realization (did we achieve the promised benefits?).
rubric.managed: A formal value realization process tracks actual benefits against business case projections for all significant investments. Reviews occur at defined intervals post-implementation (e.g., 6 months, 12 months).
rubric.optimized: Continuous value tracking is integrated with portfolio management and financial systems. Automated dashboards show real-time benefit realization. Insights from value tracking directly inform future investment decisions.

items.text: How well does the organization use lessons learned from past investments to improve future investment decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: No lessons learned process exists for IT investments. The organization repeats the same mistakes and does not benefit from past experience.
rubric.foundational: Lessons learned are captured informally for some projects but are not systematically collected, stored, or referenced in future investment decisions.
rubric.managed: A structured lessons learned program captures insights from completed investments and makes them available to future investment proposals. Investment review bodies reference past experience in their evaluations.
rubric.optimized: A knowledge management system catalogs investment outcomes, lessons, and patterns. Analytics identify systematic biases (e.g., consistent cost underestimation) and adjust evaluation models accordingly.

items.text: To what extent does the organization make course-correction decisions on underperforming IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Underperforming investments continue to receive funding due to sunk cost fallacy, organizational inertia, or lack of visibility into performance. No kill criteria or pivot mechanisms exist.
rubric.foundational: Severely underperforming investments are occasionally cancelled or restructured, but decisions are reactive and politically difficult. No formal criteria trigger investment review.
rubric.managed: Stage-gate reviews evaluate investment health at defined milestones. Predefined criteria trigger escalation for underperforming investments. Options include restructuring, descoping, or termination.
rubric.optimized: Portfolio monitoring continuously assesses investment health using earned value, risk burn-down, and benefit accrual metrics. Proactive course correction is culturally accepted and decision-supported with data.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and communicate the mission value delivered by IT investments to agency leadership and OMB?

rubric.ad_hoc: Mission value of IT investments is not measured or communicated. IT is perceived as a cost center, and investment outcomes are described in technical rather than mission terms.
rubric.foundational: Some effort is made to describe IT investment outcomes in mission terms, but measurement is qualitative and inconsistent. OMB Exhibit 300 business cases include mission alignment statements but lack quantified value metrics.
rubric.managed: Mission value metrics are defined for all major IT investments and tracked throughout the investment lifecycle. Value reporting is integrated with agency performance reporting and OMB submission processes.
rubric.optimized: Mission value measurement is comprehensive and continuous. IT investments are directly linked to agency strategic objective outcomes with quantified impact metrics. Value data drives real-time investment portfolio optimization and strengthens OMB budget justifications.

items.text: How effectively does the organization comply with OMB capital planning and investment control (CPIC) requirements for IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: CPIC processes are treated as paperwork exercises with minimal analytical rigor. Exhibit 300s and Exhibit 53s are prepared by compliance staff with limited IT or business leadership involvement.
rubric.foundational: CPIC submissions meet minimum OMB requirements but are not integrated with actual investment decision-making. Business cases prepared for CPIC may differ from internal planning documents.
rubric.managed: CPIC processes are integrated with internal investment governance. Exhibit 300 business cases reflect genuine analysis and are used to manage investments throughout their lifecycle. TechStat reviews are conducted proactively.
rubric.optimized: CPIC is a fully integrated component of investment management, not a separate compliance activity. Business cases are living documents updated with actual performance data. The organization uses CPIC frameworks to drive investment excellence beyond compliance.

items.text: To what extent does the organization evaluate IT investments for alignment with the Federal IT Acquisition Reform Act (FITARA) and related mandates?

rubric.ad_hoc: FITARA requirements are not factored into investment decisions. CIO authority over IT investments is limited or circumvented through fragmented procurement strategies.
rubric.foundational: FITARA awareness exists, and major investments receive CIO review, but the review process lacks analytical rigor. Some IT spending avoids CIO oversight through categorization as non-IT.
rubric.managed: All IT investments are subject to CIO review per FITARA requirements. A structured evaluation framework assesses investments against FITARA criteria including cost, schedule, risk, and alignment with agency IT strategy.
rubric.optimized: FITARA-compliant investment review is a strategic enabler rather than a compliance gate. The CIO office uses FITARA authority to drive portfolio optimization, eliminate duplicative investments, and ensure alignment with government-wide initiatives (e.g., Cloud Smart, Zero Trust).

items.text: How well does the organization conduct TechStat-style reviews for at-risk or underperforming IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: No structured review process exists for underperforming IT investments. Problem investments continue unchecked until they become crises or are flagged by oversight bodies.
rubric.foundational: Reviews of troubled investments occur reactively, typically in response to GAO findings or OMB inquiries. Reviews are ad hoc with no standard methodology or follow-up process.
rubric.managed: A formal TechStat review process identifies at-risk investments through established criteria (cost overruns, schedule delays, performance gaps). Reviews follow a structured methodology with documented action items and follow-up.
rubric.optimized: TechStat reviews are part of a proactive investment health monitoring system. Predictive analytics identify investments trending toward risk before problems materialize. A continuous improvement loop captures lessons learned and applies them to investment governance.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage the IT investment portfolio to reduce duplication across bureaus, programs, or mission areas?

rubric.ad_hoc: No process exists to identify or eliminate duplicative IT investments. Multiple bureaus or programs may fund similar capabilities without coordination or awareness.
rubric.foundational: Duplicative investments are identified occasionally through ad hoc reviews or audit findings, but no systematic process prevents duplication or drives consolidation.
rubric.managed: Portfolio analysis systematically identifies duplicative investments across organizational boundaries. A governance process evaluates consolidation opportunities and requires justification for continuing investments that overlap with enterprise capabilities.
rubric.optimized: Advanced portfolio analytics continuously scan for duplication using service, capability, and technology mapping. A mature governance process drives consolidation, shared service adoption, and enterprise platform development. Measurable reduction in duplicative spending is demonstrated.

items.text: How effectively does the organization define and measure business value for IT investments beyond cost savings?

rubric.ad_hoc: Business value is not defined or measured for IT investments. Success is judged solely on whether the project was delivered on time and on budget.
rubric.foundational: Business value is described qualitatively in business cases, but measurable value metrics are not defined. Post-implementation value assessment is rare.
rubric.managed: Business value metrics are defined for all significant investments, including mission outcome improvements, process efficiency gains, user experience enhancements, and risk reduction. Value realization is tracked post-implementation.
rubric.optimized: A comprehensive value management framework defines, measures, and tracks business value across the full investment lifecycle. Value realization data feeds back into future investment prioritization. The organization can demonstrate cumulative value delivered by the IT portfolio.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use structured frameworks (e.g., weighted scoring, real options analysis) for IT investment prioritization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Investment prioritization is based on executive judgment, political influence, or first-come-first-served processes. No structured framework guides resource allocation decisions.
rubric.foundational: A basic scoring framework exists (e.g., strategic alignment, risk, cost) but criteria weights are subjective and applied inconsistently. Scoring results may be overridden without documented rationale.
rubric.managed: A structured prioritization framework with defined criteria, weights, and scoring methodology is consistently applied to all investment requests. Scoring results drive resource allocation with documented exceptions.
rubric.optimized: Advanced prioritization uses multi-criteria decision analysis with validated weights, scenario modeling, and portfolio optimization algorithms. The framework balances strategic alignment, risk, value, and resource constraints to maximize portfolio value.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the IT investment pipeline from ideation through approval and into execution?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal investment pipeline exists. Investment requests enter the process through various channels without standardization, and the path from idea to funding is unclear.
rubric.foundational: A basic pipeline process exists with defined submission requirements, but the process is slow and opaque. Requestors have limited visibility into where their proposals stand or why decisions are made.
rubric.managed: A well-managed pipeline process moves investment requests through defined stages (ideation, screening, business case, approval, execution) with clear criteria and transparent decision-making at each stage.
rubric.optimized: A streamlined investment pipeline integrates with strategic planning and demand management. Digital workflow tools provide real-time pipeline visibility. Fast-track processes exist for small investments. Analytics track pipeline health metrics (throughput, cycle time, approval rates).

items.text: How effectively does the organization adapt investment governance processes for agile and iterative delivery approaches?

rubric.ad_hoc: Investment governance assumes waterfall delivery. Agile projects struggle with traditional stage-gate approval processes, funding models, and reporting requirements.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that agile delivery requires different governance, but traditional processes are applied with ad hoc exceptions. Agile teams may bypass governance entirely.
rubric.managed: Investment governance processes are adapted for agile delivery, with incremental funding decisions, outcome-based milestones, and demo-based progress reviews. Both traditional and agile governance paths are available.
rubric.optimized: Investment governance is fully agile-compatible, with modular funding, continuous value assessment, and pivot/persevere decision points. Portfolio governance seamlessly manages a mix of agile, hybrid, and traditional investments.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use minimum viable product (MVP) and lean startup approaches for IT investment decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: All IT investments require full business cases and upfront commitment of total project funding. No mechanism exists for exploratory or incremental investment approaches.
rubric.foundational: MVP concepts are discussed but rarely implemented due to governance constraints. Some innovation-labeled projects receive informal permission to experiment, but without structured approach.
rubric.managed: MVP and lean startup approaches are formally supported in the investment framework. Small funding increments enable experimentation. Structured evaluation criteria determine whether to scale, pivot, or stop.
rubric.optimized: A mature innovation investment framework provides multiple pathways from experiment to enterprise scale. Seed funding, incubation, and scaling mechanisms are well-defined. The portfolio includes a managed pipeline of experiments with clear graduation criteria.

items.text: How well does the organization balance long-term strategic investments with quick-win optimization opportunities in the IT portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: The portfolio is not managed for balance. Either long-term projects consume all resources, leaving no capacity for quick wins, or constant tactical work prevents strategic progress.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of the balance issue exists, but no portfolio mechanism ensures allocation across time horizons. Quick-win opportunities are pursued opportunistically.
rubric.managed: Portfolio governance explicitly allocates resources across time horizons (strategic, tactical, quick-win). A managed pipeline of quick-win opportunities provides near-term value while strategic investments progress on planned timelines.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic portfolio balancing continuously optimizes resource allocation across time horizons. Quick-win tracking demonstrates ongoing value delivery. Strategic investment milestones are met without sacrificing tactical improvements. Portfolio health metrics monitor balance across multiple dimensions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization apply modular contracting and incremental delivery principles to IT investments as encouraged by FITARA and OMB guidance?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT investments are primarily structured as large, monolithic programs with extended delivery timelines. Modular contracting principles are not applied, and incremental delivery is not a standard practice.
rubric.foundational: Some investments use modular approaches, but the organization defaults to traditional waterfall delivery and monolithic contracts. OMB guidance on modular contracting (M-14-03) is referenced but not consistently followed.
rubric.managed: Modular contracting and incremental delivery are standard practice for major IT investments. Contracts are structured with defined modules, off-ramps, and independent delivery milestones. TechStat-style reviews occur at increment boundaries.
rubric.optimized: Modular, incremental delivery is the default approach for all IT investments. Automated portfolio monitoring tracks delivery velocity and value realization at the module level. Underperforming modules trigger rapid corrective action, and successful patterns are codified as organizational standards.

items.text: How effectively does the organization assess and manage risk across the IT investment portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT investment risk is not systematically assessed. Individual project risks may be identified, but portfolio-level risk analysis is not performed.
rubric.foundational: Basic risk assessments are performed for major investments, but risk categories are limited (usually cost and schedule). Portfolio-level risk aggregation and correlation analysis are not performed.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive risk assessment covers all significant investments across multiple risk categories (technical, cost, schedule, organizational, security). Portfolio-level risk analysis identifies concentrations and correlations. Risk-adjusted prioritization is standard practice.
rubric.optimized: Advanced portfolio risk management uses quantitative methods (Monte Carlo, real options) to model uncertainty. Portfolio construction optimizes the risk-return profile. Continuous risk monitoring provides early warning. Risk management integrates with enterprise risk management frameworks.

items.text: To what extent does the organization monitor and manage cost and schedule performance of active IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Active IT investment performance is not monitored centrally. Cost overruns and schedule delays are discovered late, often when recovery is no longer possible.
rubric.foundational: Performance reporting exists for major investments, but data is self-reported by project teams and may be overly optimistic. Earned value or equivalent metrics are not consistently used.
rubric.managed: All significant investments are monitored using standardized performance metrics (e.g., Earned Value Management for major acquisitions per OMB A-11). Automated dashboards provide portfolio-level performance visibility. Variances trigger defined escalation procedures.
rubric.optimized: Predictive performance analytics identify investments trending toward trouble before variances exceed thresholds. Automated data collection eliminates reporting bias. Portfolio performance benchmarks enable comparison across investment types and time periods. Continuous improvement of investment execution effectiveness is demonstrated.

items.text: How well does the organization learn from completed IT investments to improve future investment decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: No post-implementation review process exists. Lessons from successful and failed investments are not captured or applied to future decisions.
rubric.foundational: Post-implementation reviews are conducted occasionally for high-profile investments, but lessons learned are not systematically captured or disseminated.
rubric.managed: Post-implementation reviews are standard practice for all significant investments. Lessons learned are documented, categorized, and made available to future investment teams. Patterns across reviews inform governance process improvements.
rubric.optimized: A comprehensive learning system captures, analyzes, and applies lessons from all investment outcomes. Pattern analysis identifies systemic issues in estimation, execution, or governance. Predictive models improve based on outcome data. The organization demonstrates measurable improvement in investment success rates over time.

items.text: How well does the organization assess and manage the risk of IT investment portfolio concentration, including over-reliance on single vendors, technologies, or delivery models?

rubric.ad_hoc: Portfolio concentration risk is not assessed. The organization may be heavily dependent on single vendors or technologies without awareness of the associated risks.
rubric.foundational: Some awareness of concentration risk exists (e.g., major vendor dependency is acknowledged), but no formal assessment or mitigation strategy is in place.
rubric.managed: Portfolio concentration risk is formally assessed across dimensions including vendor dependency, technology platform risk, skill concentration, and delivery model reliance. Mitigation strategies are developed for identified concentrations.
rubric.optimized: Continuous portfolio risk monitoring includes automated concentration analysis with defined thresholds and mitigation triggers. Diversification strategies are embedded in investment planning, and concentration metrics are reported to investment governance boards.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT contract lifecycle management process (from solicitation through closeout)?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT contracts are managed ad hoc with no standardized process. Contract documents may be stored in individual email or file shares. Key dates (renewals, expirations) are tracked informally, leading to missed deadlines and auto-renewals.
rubric.foundational: A basic contract management process exists with templates and a central repository, but lifecycle activities (renewal planning, performance review, closeout) are not consistently performed.
rubric.managed: A structured contract lifecycle process covers all phases: requirements, solicitation, award, performance monitoring, modification, renewal/re-compete, and closeout. Automated alerts ensure timely action on key dates.
rubric.optimized: Contract lifecycle management is fully automated with workflow-driven processes, integrated with financial and performance systems. Analytics identify optimization opportunities (e.g., consolidation, renegotiation timing). Lessons learned inform future acquisitions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage IT contract renewals and renegotiations to optimize cost and terms?

rubric.ad_hoc: Contract renewals are managed reactively, often resulting in auto-renewals at unfavorable terms. Renegotiation opportunities are missed due to late awareness of expiration dates.
rubric.foundational: Contract expiration tracking prevents auto-renewals, but renegotiation is limited to price discussions without leveraging market data, consumption trends, or competitive alternatives.
rubric.managed: Renewal planning begins 6-12 months before expiration with structured evaluation of options (renew, renegotiate, re-compete, consolidate). Market data and consumption analysis inform negotiation strategy.
rubric.optimized: Continuous contract optimization identifies renegotiation opportunities throughout the contract lifecycle, not just at renewal. Predictive analytics forecast optimal negotiation timing. Strategic vendor relationships enable collaborative value improvement.

items.text: How well does the organization maintain visibility into IT contract spend, terms, and obligations across the contract portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: No consolidated view of IT contracts exists. Contract information is scattered across files, email, and procurement systems. Total contract spend by vendor is difficult to determine.
rubric.foundational: A contract repository exists but may be incomplete. Basic spend data is available by vendor, but detailed terms, obligations, and SLA commitments are not consistently captured in a searchable format.
rubric.managed: A comprehensive contract management system captures all IT contracts with key terms, financial commitments, SLAs, and obligations. Dashboards provide portfolio-level visibility into spend, expiration timelines, and risk.
rubric.optimized: AI-assisted contract analytics extract and monitor terms, obligations, and compliance requirements across the portfolio. Real-time spend tracking identifies variance from contracted rates. Risk scoring prioritizes contract management attention.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage contract transition and knowledge transfer when changing vendors or bringing services in-house?

rubric.ad_hoc: Contract transitions are poorly managed with significant knowledge loss and service disruptions. No formal transition planning or knowledge transfer requirements exist in contracts.
rubric.foundational: Basic transition clauses exist in some contracts, but knowledge transfer activities are ad hoc and incomplete. Service disruptions during vendor transitions are common.
rubric.managed: Formal transition management plans are required for all significant contracts. Knowledge transfer activities are defined, scheduled, and monitored. Transition readiness assessments validate preparedness before vendor changes occur.
rubric.optimized: Contract transitions are seamlessly managed through comprehensive transition playbooks, automated knowledge repositories, and competitive transition incentive structures. Zero-disruption transitions are the norm, and transition lessons learned are institutionalized.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and manage IT vendor performance?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor performance is not formally measured. Issues are addressed reactively when problems become severe. No performance metrics or scorecards exist for IT vendors.
rubric.foundational: Basic performance metrics are tracked for some critical vendors (e.g., SLA compliance, incident counts) but monitoring is inconsistent and scorecards are not regularly reviewed with vendors.
rubric.managed: A formal vendor performance management program includes defined metrics, regular scorecards, and structured review meetings for all significant vendors. Performance data informs contract renewal and expansion decisions.
rubric.optimized: Vendor performance management is data-driven and continuous, with automated monitoring, predictive risk indicators, and integration with contract and financial management. Performance trends inform strategic sourcing decisions.

items.text: How well does the organization manage strategic vendor relationships beyond transactional contract compliance?

rubric.ad_hoc: All vendor relationships are transactional, focused on contract compliance and issue resolution. No distinction is made between strategic and commodity vendors.
rubric.foundational: Key vendors are identified as strategic, but relationship management beyond contract meetings is limited. Innovation and partnership discussions are rare.
rubric.managed: A vendor segmentation model guides different levels of relationship management. Strategic vendors have executive sponsors, innovation agendas, joint roadmaps, and regular strategic reviews.
rubric.optimized: Strategic vendor management drives mutual value creation through joint innovation, risk sharing, and co-investment. Vendor ecosystem management optimizes the overall vendor portfolio for cost, capability, risk, and innovation.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage concentration risk and dependency across its IT vendor portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor concentration risk is not assessed. The organization may be heavily dependent on a single vendor for critical services without awareness of the risk.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of major vendor dependencies exists, but concentration risk is not formally assessed or managed. No criteria exist for acceptable levels of vendor concentration.
rubric.managed: Vendor concentration risk is assessed regularly, considering spend, criticality, substitutability, and financial viability. Risk thresholds are defined and mitigation strategies exist for high-concentration areas.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic vendor risk monitoring includes financial health, geopolitical, cyber, and operational risk factors. Scenario planning evaluates vendor failure impacts. Multi-sourcing strategies are optimized to balance cost efficiency and risk resilience.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use contractor performance data from CPARS and other sources to inform future IT procurement decisions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Past performance data is not systematically used in IT procurement decisions. CPARS entries may be incomplete, outdated, or not consulted during source selection.
rubric.foundational: CPARS data is reviewed during major source selections, but evaluations may not cover all relevant past contracts. The organization's own CPARS submissions may be incomplete or delayed.
rubric.managed: Past performance evaluation is a robust component of IT source selection, drawing on CPARS, internal performance data, and references. The organization maintains complete, timely CPARS records for all IT contracts.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive past performance intelligence combines CPARS data, internal performance metrics, and cross-government information to inform procurement decisions. The organization is recognized for thorough, timely CPARS reporting and uses performance data to build a preferred vendor ecosystem.

items.text: How well does the organization use vendor performance data to inform re-competition decisions and future acquisition strategies?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor performance data is not maintained in a form that supports re-competition or future acquisition decisions. Past vendor performance is not a factor in source selection.
rubric.foundational: Basic vendor performance information is available for major contracts, but it is not systematically organized or accessible to acquisition teams. Past performance evaluations in CPARS are completed but not strategically used.
rubric.managed: Vendor performance data is centrally maintained and accessible to acquisition teams. CPARS evaluations are thorough and timely, and past performance is a significant factor in source selection per FAR Part 42.
rubric.optimized: Vendor performance analytics drive acquisition strategy. Predictive models assess vendor risk and value based on comprehensive performance histories. Performance data informs total evaluated price models and enables strategic vendor portfolio management.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage software license compliance and optimization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Software license management is informal. The organization cannot accurately determine its license entitlements vs. actual usage, creating compliance risk and cost waste.
rubric.foundational: Basic license tracking exists for major software vendors, but reconciliation of entitlements vs. deployments is infrequent and incomplete. Compliance audits reveal surprises.
rubric.managed: A software asset management program tracks entitlements, deployments, and usage across major software vendors. Regular reconciliation identifies compliance gaps and optimization opportunities (unused licenses, downgrade opportunities).
rubric.optimized: Automated software asset management tools continuously reconcile entitlements and usage across all vendors and deployment models (on-premises, SaaS, cloud). Optimization recommendations are generated automatically, and license harvesting is routine.

items.text: How well does the organization optimize its software licensing models to minimize cost while meeting business needs?

rubric.ad_hoc: Licensing models are selected based on vendor default recommendations without analysis of alternatives. The organization may be over-licensed, using expensive editions for basic needs.
rubric.foundational: Some analysis of licensing alternatives is performed during major renewals, but optimization is not systematic. The organization may not be aware of all available licensing models.
rubric.managed: Licensing model optimization is a structured practice, evaluating options (perpetual vs. subscription, enterprise vs. per-user, cloud vs. on-premises) for all major software spend. Analysis considers total cost across the license lifecycle.
rubric.optimized: Continuous licensing optimization leverages usage analytics, market intelligence, and vendor negotiations to minimize cost while maintaining compliance. Licensing strategy is proactively adapted to changing technology delivery models.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage software license compliance risk, particularly for complex enterprise agreements?

rubric.ad_hoc: License compliance is not proactively managed. The organization discovers compliance issues during vendor audits, often resulting in significant true-up costs and penalties.
rubric.foundational: Compliance is checked periodically for the largest software vendors, but complex licensing terms (e.g., core-based, processor-based, virtualization rights) may not be fully understood or tracked.
rubric.managed: A proactive compliance management program monitors all significant software agreements, with staff trained on complex licensing terms. Self-audits are performed regularly, and findings are remediated before vendor audits.
rubric.optimized: Automated compliance monitoring continuously tracks deployments against entitlements for all licensing models. Expert knowledge of licensing terms is maintained and applied to architectural decisions. The organization is consistently audit-ready.

items.text: How well does the organization negotiate and manage enterprise license agreements (ELAs) to optimize cost and ensure compliance across the organization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Enterprise license agreements are negotiated independently by business units. No coordination exists to leverage aggregate purchasing power, and compliance monitoring is minimal.
rubric.foundational: Some coordination of enterprise license negotiations occurs through the procurement office, but terms are not optimized for agency-wide needs. License compliance is checked periodically but not systematically.
rubric.managed: Enterprise license agreements are strategically negotiated with agency-wide requirements analysis and volume leverage. License management tools track compliance, utilization, and cost-effectiveness across all enterprise agreements.
rubric.optimized: ELA management is a strategic capability with continuous optimization of terms, pricing, and deployment rights. Automated license management platforms ensure compliance, identify savings opportunities, and support government-wide purchasing vehicles like GSA BPAs and SEWP.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage strategic vendor relationships beyond transactional contract management?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor relationships are purely transactional, focused on contract compliance and invoice processing. No distinction is made between strategic and commodity vendors.
rubric.foundational: A few key vendors are recognized as strategic, but relationship management is limited to periodic meetings. No formal framework guides strategic vendor engagement.
rubric.managed: A vendor tiering model classifies vendors by strategic importance. Strategic vendors have dedicated relationship managers, executive engagement, joint planning sessions, and performance dashboards.
rubric.optimized: Strategic vendor management is a mature discipline with executive sponsorship, innovation partnerships, joint roadmap planning, and shared risk/reward models. Vendor relationships are evaluated for strategic value and regularly rebalanced.

items.text: To what extent does the organization leverage government-wide and multi-agency contract vehicles (e.g., GWACs, BPAs, GSA Schedule) to optimize IT procurement costs?

rubric.ad_hoc: Government-wide contract vehicles are used inconsistently. Procurement staff may not be aware of available vehicles, leading to duplicative standalone contracts at higher costs.
rubric.foundational: Major government-wide vehicles (GSA Schedule, GWACs) are used for common purchases, but strategic use of these vehicles for cost optimization is limited. Best-in-class pricing is not consistently pursued.
rubric.managed: The organization strategically uses government-wide and multi-agency contract vehicles to optimize costs, reduce procurement cycle times, and leverage collective buying power. Contract vehicle selection is analyzed for each major acquisition.
rubric.optimized: The organization is a leader in strategic use of government contract vehicles, contributing to vehicle governance and leveraging data analytics to identify best-value procurement paths. Category management principles guide all IT acquisition strategies per OMB M-19-13.

items.text: How well does the organization manage vendor concentration risk and ensure healthy competition in IT procurement?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor concentration is not monitored. The organization may be overly dependent on a small number of vendors without awareness of the risk, or locked into single-source arrangements.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of vendor concentration exists for the largest contracts, but no systematic analysis evaluates concentration risk across the IT portfolio. Competitive alternatives are not proactively cultivated.
rubric.managed: Vendor concentration risk is systematically assessed across the IT portfolio. Procurement strategies include market research to ensure competition. Risk mitigation plans exist for critical single-source dependencies.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive vendor portfolio analysis monitors concentration, competition health, and market dynamics. Proactive vendor development cultivates alternatives for critical capabilities. Contract structures ensure portability and prevent lock-in. Vendor diversity goals align with socioeconomic requirements.

items.text: How effectively does the organization develop and maintain strategic vendor partnerships that extend beyond transactional contracting to drive innovation and mission value?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor relationships are purely transactional. Interactions are limited to contract compliance and invoice processing with no strategic engagement or innovation collaboration.
rubric.foundational: Some strategic dialogue occurs with major vendors, but relationships lack structure. Innovation discussions are opportunistic rather than programmatic, and partnership commitments are informal.
rubric.managed: Strategic vendor partnerships are formally structured with defined objectives, governance cadences, and innovation agendas. Partnership reviews assess mutual value creation and alignment with agency strategic priorities.
rubric.optimized: Strategic partnerships drive significant mission innovation and value creation. Formal innovation funds, joint development agreements, and shared roadmap planning enable vendors to contribute to mission outcomes beyond contract deliverables. Partnership ROI is measured and reported.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage cloud service provider relationships and contracts?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud service contracts are managed ad hoc, often by individual project teams rather than centralized procurement or vendor management. Contract terms may not adequately protect government interests.
rubric.foundational: Centralized awareness of cloud contracts exists, but management practices developed for traditional IT procurement are applied without adaptation to cloud-specific considerations (elastic pricing, data sovereignty, exit planning).
rubric.managed: Cloud vendor management addresses cloud-specific concerns including consumption optimization, reserved capacity commitments, data residency, FedRAMP compliance, and exit/portability planning. Cloud enterprise agreements are centrally managed.
rubric.optimized: Mature cloud vendor management integrates FinOps, security compliance, and strategic planning. Multi-cloud governance optimizes across providers. Contract structures maximize flexibility while protecting government interests. Cloud vendor performance is benchmarked continuously.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage SaaS subscription sprawl and optimize SaaS spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: SaaS subscriptions are not centrally tracked. Individual teams procure SaaS products independently, leading to duplicate subscriptions, unused licenses, and unmanaged data risks.
rubric.foundational: A partial inventory of SaaS subscriptions exists, but shadow SaaS is prevalent. License utilization is not monitored, and renewal decisions are made without consumption analysis.
rubric.managed: SaaS subscriptions are centrally inventoried and governed. License utilization is monitored, and underutilized subscriptions are identified for right-sizing or cancellation. Renewal decisions are informed by usage data.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive SaaS management includes automated discovery, utilization analytics, spend optimization, and lifecycle governance. The organization consolidates overlapping SaaS products, negotiates enterprise agreements where beneficial, and maintains a SaaS rationalization roadmap.

items.text: How well does the organization evaluate and manage vendor lock-in risks for cloud and SaaS investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor lock-in risk is not considered in cloud or SaaS procurement decisions. Applications and data become deeply embedded in vendor-specific platforms without portability planning.
rubric.foundational: Lock-in risk is acknowledged in procurement discussions but not systematically assessed. Exit clauses in contracts may be weak or absent. Data portability requirements are not defined.
rubric.managed: Lock-in risk assessment is a standard part of cloud and SaaS procurement evaluation. Contracts include data portability, API access, and exit assistance provisions. Architecture standards promote portability where feasible.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive lock-in risk management includes contract provisions, architecture standards, regular portability testing, and exit cost modeling. The organization maintains viable alternatives for critical cloud services and can execute provider transitions with managed cost and risk.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage SaaS vendor lock-in risk and ensure data portability across cloud service providers?

rubric.ad_hoc: SaaS vendor lock-in risk is not assessed. Data portability requirements are not included in contracts, and the organization has no strategy for migrating away from incumbent SaaS providers.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of lock-in risk exists, but data portability and interoperability requirements are inconsistently included in SaaS contracts. Migration feasibility has not been assessed for existing SaaS services.
rubric.managed: Data portability and interoperability requirements are standard in all SaaS contracts. Lock-in risk is assessed during acquisition and monitored during contract performance. Migration plans exist for critical SaaS services.
rubric.optimized: Lock-in risk management is integrated into cloud strategy with automated multi-cloud portability testing, contractual exit provisions aligned with FedRAMP standards, and active management of data formats and API dependencies. The organization can migrate critical services with minimal disruption.

items.text: How effectively does the organization apply category management principles to IT acquisitions per OMB guidance?

rubric.ad_hoc: Category management is not applied to IT acquisitions. Each procurement is treated independently without consideration of spend category optimization or government-wide best practices.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of category management exists, but IT acquisitions are not systematically organized into spend categories. Best-in-class (BIC) contract vehicle usage is not tracked.
rubric.managed: IT acquisitions are organized into spend categories with designated category leads. BIC vehicle usage is tracked and increasing. Category strategies address common buys (laptops, cloud, staffing services).
rubric.optimized: Mature category management drives all IT acquisitions. Category strategies optimize across the enterprise, leveraging government-wide data and BIC vehicles per OMB M-19-13. The organization contributes to government-wide category management as a leader.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use data analytics to inform IT procurement strategy and vendor negotiations?

rubric.ad_hoc: Procurement decisions are made without analytical support. Historical spending data, market intelligence, and vendor performance data are not used to inform acquisition strategy.
rubric.foundational: Basic spending analysis is performed for major procurements, but comprehensive analytics covering market conditions, historical pricing, and vendor performance are not available.
rubric.managed: Data analytics inform procurement strategy, including spend analysis, market research, should-cost modeling, and historical vendor performance. Analytics drive negotiation preparation and contract structure decisions.
rubric.optimized: Advanced procurement analytics use AI/ML to optimize acquisition strategy. Predictive models forecast market conditions and pricing trends. Real-time dashboards track procurement pipeline health. Analytics drive continuous improvement of acquisition outcomes and cost savings.

items.text: How well does the organization manage IT procurement cycle times to ensure timely acquisition of needed capabilities?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT procurement cycle times are not measured or managed. Acquisitions frequently take 12-18 months or longer, delaying mission-critical capabilities.
rubric.foundational: Procurement cycle times are measured for some acquisitions, but no improvement targets exist. Long cycle times are accepted as inherent to government procurement.
rubric.managed: Procurement cycle times are tracked by acquisition type and size, with improvement targets and root cause analysis for delays. Streamlined processes (e.g., blanket purchase agreements, simplified acquisitions) reduce cycle times for common buys.
rubric.optimized: Procurement cycle time optimization is a strategic priority. The organization consistently achieves below-average cycle times through process innovation, pre-positioned vehicles, and acquisition workforce development. Cycle time metrics are benchmarked against peer agencies.

items.text: How well does the organization leverage government-wide acquisition vehicles and shared services to improve IT procurement efficiency and achieve best value?

rubric.ad_hoc: Government-wide acquisition vehicles are underutilized. Procurement teams default to standalone contracts without assessing whether existing vehicles (e.g., GSA Schedule, SEWP, CIO-SP3) offer better value or faster acquisition.
rubric.foundational: Government-wide vehicles are used for some commodity purchases, but strategic use is limited. Procurement teams are not fully trained on available vehicles or best practices for their use.
rubric.managed: Strategic use of government-wide vehicles is standard practice. Procurement teams evaluate available vehicles for all acquisitions and select the approach that offers best value, speed, and compliance. Vehicle utilization is tracked and reported.
rubric.optimized: Optimized use of government-wide vehicles and category management principles drives procurement efficiency. The organization actively participates in government-wide category management initiatives, shares best practices, and achieves measurable savings through strategic sourcing.

items.text: How effectively does the organization assess and manage supply chain risk for IT vendors, including cybersecurity and foreign ownership risks?

rubric.ad_hoc: Supply chain risk for IT vendors is not assessed. The organization does not evaluate vendors for cybersecurity practices, foreign ownership, or supply chain integrity as required by federal directives.
rubric.foundational: Basic supply chain risk screening occurs for some procurements (e.g., Section 889 compliance), but comprehensive risk assessment covering cybersecurity, foreign ownership, and component sourcing is not performed.
rubric.managed: Supply chain risk management is integrated into IT procurement with structured assessments covering cybersecurity practices, foreign ownership (FOCI), component sourcing, and compliance with applicable federal directives (EO 13873, Section 889). Risk mitigation plans address identified concerns.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive supply chain risk management uses threat intelligence, continuous monitoring, and advanced analytics to assess and mitigate vendor risks. The organization participates in government-wide information sharing and contributes to FASC supply chain risk management activities. Risk assessments are updated throughout the vendor relationship.

items.text: To what extent does the organization monitor vendor compliance with federal requirements (FedRAMP, FISMA, Section 508, labor standards)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Vendor compliance with federal requirements is not monitored after contract award. Non-compliance may persist undetected, creating legal, security, and accessibility risks.
rubric.foundational: Compliance requirements are included in contracts, but monitoring is limited to self-attestation by vendors. The organization lacks the capability to independently verify compliance.
rubric.managed: Vendor compliance is actively monitored through regular assessments, evidence reviews, and audit rights exercises. FedRAMP authorization status, Section 508 conformance, and FISMA compliance are tracked and reported.
rubric.optimized: Automated compliance monitoring continuously tracks vendor adherence to federal requirements. Integration with FedRAMP PMO, DHS CDM, and agency assessment processes provides comprehensive compliance visibility. Non-compliance triggers immediate remediation workflows.

items.text: How well does the organization manage vendor transition and exit planning to ensure continuity of service and data protection?

rubric.ad_hoc: No vendor exit planning exists. Vendor transitions are reactive and disruptive, with data migration, knowledge transfer, and service continuity handled in crisis mode.
rubric.foundational: Exit planning is addressed in contracts for major vendors, but plans are generic and not tested. Data portability and transition assistance provisions may be weak or absent.
rubric.managed: Vendor exit plans are developed for all significant vendor relationships, addressing service continuity, data migration, knowledge transfer, and transition timelines. Plans are reviewed and updated annually.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive exit planning includes tested transition playbooks, escrowed data and documentation, pre-negotiated transition assistance provisions, and alternative vendor qualification. Exit readiness is a factor in ongoing vendor risk assessment.

items.text: How comprehensive and accurate is the organization's IT asset inventory?

rubric.ad_hoc: No centralized IT asset inventory exists. Asset information is fragmented across spreadsheets, emails, and individual knowledge. The organization cannot produce an accurate count of its IT assets.
rubric.foundational: A centralized asset inventory exists but is incomplete, covering only some asset types (e.g., desktops but not network equipment, or hardware but not cloud resources). Accuracy degrades over time without regular updates.
rubric.managed: A comprehensive asset inventory covers all significant IT assets including hardware, software, cloud resources, and mobile devices. Automated discovery tools maintain accuracy. Regular reconciliation ensures completeness.
rubric.optimized: Real-time asset visibility is maintained through continuous automated discovery and reconciliation across all environments (on-premises, cloud, mobile, IoT). The inventory is a trusted source for financial, security, and operational decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use automated discovery tools to maintain asset inventory accuracy?

rubric.ad_hoc: No automated discovery tools are deployed. Asset inventory updates rely entirely on manual data entry, which is error-prone and quickly becomes outdated.
rubric.foundational: Some automated discovery is in place (e.g., network scanning, Active Directory) but coverage gaps exist. Discovery results are not automatically reconciled with the asset management system.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive automated discovery covers all major asset types and environments. Discovery data is automatically fed into the asset management system with reconciliation rules to handle conflicts and exceptions.
rubric.optimized: Multi-source discovery integrates network scanning, agent-based collection, cloud APIs, and procurement feeds into a unified asset record. AI-assisted reconciliation handles complex scenarios. Coverage gaps are automatically identified and reported.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track financial attributes of IT assets (acquisition cost, depreciation, maintenance cost, residual value)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Financial attributes of IT assets are maintained only in the financial system (fixed asset register) with no link to the operational asset inventory. IT cannot determine the cost of assets they manage.
rubric.foundational: Acquisition costs are captured for some assets, but depreciation, maintenance, and disposal costs are not tracked in the asset management system. Financial and IT asset records are not reconciled.
rubric.managed: Financial attributes including acquisition cost, depreciation schedule, maintenance cost, and warranty status are tracked in the asset management system and reconciled with the fixed asset register.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive financial lifecycle tracking is fully automated, covering acquisition, operating costs, depreciation, and disposal value. Asset financial data feeds directly into TCO calculations and service costing models.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain an accurate inventory of IT assets for cybersecurity purposes, aligned with BOD 23-01 and CDM requirements?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset inventory is insufficient for cybersecurity purposes. The organization cannot produce a complete, accurate list of networked assets within required timeframes for vulnerability management or incident response.
rubric.foundational: A basic IT asset inventory exists, but gaps in coverage (especially IoT, OT, and cloud assets) and data staleness limit its utility for cybersecurity. Alignment with BOD 23-01 automated discovery requirements is incomplete.
rubric.managed: The IT asset inventory meets BOD 23-01 requirements for automated discovery with defined scanning cadences. All networked assets are inventoried including IoT, OT, and cloud. Inventory data feeds vulnerability management and CDM processes.
rubric.optimized: Continuous, automated asset discovery provides a real-time, comprehensive inventory of all networked assets. Full integration with CDM tools enables automated vulnerability assessment and compliance monitoring. The inventory supports zero trust architecture by providing authoritative device identity data.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain a comprehensive and accurate inventory of IT assets including hardware, software, and cloud subscriptions across all environments?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset inventory is incomplete and unreliable. Significant gaps exist in tracking hardware, software licenses, and cloud subscriptions. Asset discovery is manual and infrequent.
rubric.foundational: A basic asset inventory exists for on-premises hardware and major software, but cloud resources and SaaS subscriptions are not comprehensively tracked. Inventory accuracy is estimated at less than 80%.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive asset inventory covers hardware, software, cloud resources, and SaaS subscriptions across all environments. Automated discovery tools maintain accuracy above 95%. The inventory integrates with the CMDB and financial systems.
rubric.optimized: Real-time asset visibility across all environments is achieved through automated continuous discovery, reconciliation, and lifecycle tracking. Asset data is the authoritative source for financial planning, security compliance, and capacity management.

items.text: How mature is the organization's IT asset lifecycle management process (procurement through disposal)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Assets are procured, deployed, and disposed of without a formal lifecycle process. End-of-life assets may continue in production, and disposed assets may not be properly tracked or sanitized.
rubric.foundational: Basic lifecycle tracking exists for major asset categories (e.g., standard desktop refresh cycles) but is not comprehensive. End-of-life management is reactive rather than planned.
rubric.managed: A formal asset lifecycle process covers all phases: planning, procurement, deployment, maintenance, refresh, and disposal. Lifecycle policies define standard useful life periods and refresh criteria.
rubric.optimized: Predictive lifecycle management uses asset health data, usage patterns, and failure analytics to optimize refresh timing. Automated workflows manage transitions between lifecycle phases. Disposal includes verified data sanitization and environmental compliance.

items.text: How effectively does the organization plan and execute technology refresh cycles?

rubric.ad_hoc: No planned refresh cycles exist. Assets are replaced only when they fail or become unusable. No budget is set aside for planned replacement.
rubric.foundational: Refresh cycles are defined for some asset types (e.g., 4-year desktop replacement) but are not consistently executed due to budget constraints or poor planning.
rubric.managed: Refresh cycles are defined for all major asset types, budgeted in advance, and executed on schedule. Refresh plans consider total cost of ownership, including rising maintenance costs for aging assets.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic refresh planning uses asset condition monitoring, performance data, and cost optimization models to determine ideal replacement timing for each asset. Refresh spending is smoothed to avoid spike years.

items.text: How well does the organization manage end-of-support and end-of-life risks for IT assets?

rubric.ad_hoc: End-of-support and end-of-life dates are not tracked. The organization runs unsupported hardware and software without awareness of the security and operational risks.
rubric.foundational: End-of-support dates are tracked for some critical assets, but no systematic process exists to identify and remediate all end-of-life assets before support expires.
rubric.managed: End-of-support and end-of-life dates are tracked for all assets with automated alerts. Remediation plans are developed 12+ months before support expiration. Risk-based exceptions are documented and approved.
rubric.optimized: Proactive lifecycle intelligence identifies end-of-support risks across the entire asset portfolio, including embedded components and dependencies. Vendor lifecycle roadmaps inform strategic planning. Zero end-of-support assets in production without approved risk acceptance.

items.text: How well does the organization manage end-of-life and end-of-support transitions for IT assets to minimize security risk and operational disruption?

rubric.ad_hoc: End-of-life and end-of-support dates are not tracked. Assets continue to operate beyond vendor support windows, creating security vulnerabilities and compliance risks without organizational awareness.
rubric.foundational: End-of-life dates are tracked for major assets, but transition planning is reactive. Assets are sometimes discovered to be unsupported only when security vulnerabilities are reported or patches are unavailable.
rubric.managed: End-of-life/end-of-support tracking is comprehensive with automated alerting at defined lead times (e.g., 12, 6, 3 months). Migration and replacement plans are developed proactively, and unsupported assets are tracked as risk items.
rubric.optimized: Lifecycle management is fully proactive with automated transition planning triggered by vendor roadmap integration. Zero unsupported assets operate in production. End-of-life transitions are seamlessly orchestrated with minimal mission impact.

items.text: How effectively does the organization identify and eliminate underutilized or redundant IT assets?

rubric.ad_hoc: No process exists to identify underutilized assets. Servers, software licenses, and other resources may sit idle consuming budget without detection.
rubric.foundational: Occasional reviews identify obvious waste (e.g., unused servers, unassigned licenses) but assessments are manual, infrequent, and not comprehensive.
rubric.managed: Regular utilization assessments identify underutilized and redundant assets across all major categories. Findings drive consolidation and decommission actions with tracked savings.
rubric.optimized: Continuous automated utilization monitoring identifies optimization opportunities in real time. Right-sizing recommendations are generated automatically. Reclamation and consolidation are routine practices with measurable financial impact.

items.text: To what extent does the organization perform application rationalization to optimize its application portfolio?

rubric.ad_hoc: No application rationalization has been performed. The organization's application portfolio has grown organically with significant redundancy and unknown total scope.
rubric.foundational: An initial application inventory exists, and the organization is aware of redundancies, but rationalization efforts are limited to high-profile cases (e.g., post-merger consolidation).
rubric.managed: Regular application rationalization evaluates the portfolio using criteria such as business value, technical health, cost, and redundancy. A rationalization roadmap prioritizes consolidation and retirement actions.
rubric.optimized: Continuous application portfolio management uses automated assessment, business value scoring, and total cost analysis. Rationalization is an ongoing practice integrated with investment governance and modernization planning.

items.text: How well does the organization optimize infrastructure utilization through virtualization, consolidation, and cloud migration?

rubric.ad_hoc: Infrastructure is predominantly physical with low utilization rates. No systematic effort to consolidate, virtualize, or migrate to cloud. Server sprawl is common.
rubric.foundational: Virtualization is adopted for some workloads, but utilization optimization is not systematic. Cloud migration decisions are made project-by-project without portfolio-level strategy.
rubric.managed: Infrastructure optimization is a formal program covering virtualization, right-sizing, consolidation, and cloud migration. Utilization targets are set and monitored. A cloud migration strategy guides workload placement decisions.
rubric.optimized: Automated infrastructure optimization continuously right-sizes resources across on-premises and cloud environments. Intelligent workload placement maximizes cost efficiency and performance. The organization achieves industry-leading utilization rates.
