items.text: How systematically does the organization evaluate application portfolio health and identify candidates for modernization, consolidation, or retirement?

rubric.ad_hoc: No systematic application rationalization occurs. The application portfolio grows organically with redundant and obsolete applications consuming resources indefinitely.
rubric.foundational: Periodic application reviews identify obvious retirement candidates, but evaluation criteria are informal. The organization lacks a comprehensive view of application portfolio health or rationalization priorities.
rubric.managed: Formal application rationalization uses structured evaluation criteria (business value, technical health, cost, risk) applied to the full application portfolio. Rationalization reviews produce prioritized modernization and retirement recommendations aligned with agency modernization plans.
rubric.optimized: Continuous application portfolio optimization uses automated health scoring, user adoption analytics, and cost-effectiveness metrics. Rationalization is embedded in portfolio governance, and retirement savings are reinvested in modernization per MGT Act and agency IT modernization plans.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage software licenses to ensure compliance and optimize costs?

rubric.ad_hoc: Software licenses are not centrally tracked. The organization has limited visibility into license entitlements, deployments, and compliance status. Over- and under-licensing are both likely.
rubric.foundational: A software license inventory exists for major products, but is maintained manually and may be incomplete. True-up exercises are reactive, often triggered by vendor audits.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive software asset management (SAM) covers all significant software products. License entitlements are tracked against deployments using SAM tools. Proactive compliance monitoring prevents audit surprises and identifies optimization opportunities.
rubric.optimized: Automated SAM provides real-time visibility into license compliance and optimization opportunities across all software products. Predictive analytics anticipate licensing needs. The organization consistently achieves optimal licensing positions and demonstrates savings from SAM program operations.

items.text: To what extent does the organization manage enterprise software agreements and volume licensing to minimize total cost?

rubric.ad_hoc: Enterprise agreements are negotiated without comprehensive usage data. The organization may be over-committed on enterprise licenses or paying retail prices where enterprise agreements would be more cost-effective.
rubric.foundational: Enterprise agreements exist for major software vendors, but negotiation is based on rough estimates rather than detailed consumption analysis. True-up costs are unpredictable.
rubric.managed: Enterprise agreement negotiations are informed by detailed usage analytics, growth projections, and pricing benchmarks. The organization evaluates agreement structures (enterprise, subscription, pay-per-use) to optimize total cost.
rubric.optimized: Strategic software portfolio management optimizes across all licensing models. Analytics-driven negotiations consistently achieve below-benchmark pricing. The organization leverages government-wide agreements where available and influences government buying pool negotiations.

items.text: How well does the organization manage software end-of-life and end-of-support transitions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Software end-of-life dates are not tracked. The organization discovers end-of-support situations reactively, sometimes after security vulnerabilities are no longer patched.
rubric.foundational: End-of-life dates are tracked for major software products, but migration planning is often delayed until support expiration is imminent, resulting in rushed transitions or extended support costs.
rubric.managed: Software lifecycle management includes proactive tracking of end-of-life dates, planned migration timelines, and budgeted transition costs. Migration planning begins well in advance of support expiration.
rubric.optimized: Automated lifecycle tracking monitors end-of-life status for all software assets. Coordinated migration roadmaps align with technology strategy and budget cycles. The organization avoids extended support costs through timely, planned transitions and actively manages technical debt.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage software license compliance and optimization to avoid audit risk and minimize unnecessary spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: Software license compliance is not actively managed. The organization is at risk of vendor audit findings, and significant over-licensing or under-licensing likely exists without awareness.
rubric.foundational: Basic license compliance checks are performed for major software vendors, but comprehensive reconciliation of entitlements vs. installations is not performed. Optimization is limited to obvious surplus licenses.
rubric.managed: Software license management includes automated discovery, entitlement tracking, and compliance monitoring for all significant software products. Regular optimization reviews identify opportunities to harvest unused licenses and right-size agreements.
rubric.optimized: Enterprise software asset management is fully mature with automated compliance monitoring, proactive optimization, and predictive license demand modeling. License intelligence informs all procurement decisions, and the organization consistently passes vendor audits with no material findings.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage cloud resource assets (instances, storage, databases) alongside traditional on-premises assets?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud resources are not managed as assets. Provisioned cloud instances, storage volumes, and databases are not tracked, tagged, or governed as part of the asset inventory.
rubric.foundational: Cloud resources are partially tracked, but management practices are separate from on-premises asset management. No unified view of hybrid infrastructure assets exists.
rubric.managed: Cloud resources are fully integrated into the asset management framework with consistent tagging, ownership, and lifecycle management. A unified view covers on-premises, cloud IaaS, and PaaS resources.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive hybrid asset management provides real-time visibility into all infrastructure assets regardless of deployment model. Automated governance enforces tagging, ownership, and lifecycle policies. Analytics optimize asset utilization across hybrid environments.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track and optimize utilization of cloud resources to eliminate waste?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud resource utilization is not monitored. Provisioned resources may sit idle or dramatically underutilized without detection, wasting budget.
rubric.foundational: Basic utilization reports from cloud providers are reviewed periodically, but systematic identification and remediation of underutilized resources is not performed.
rubric.managed: Cloud resource utilization is actively monitored with defined thresholds for action. Right-sizing recommendations are generated and acted upon regularly. Idle resources are identified and terminated or scheduled.
rubric.optimized: Automated utilization optimization continuously right-sizes resources, manages scheduling, and recommends architectural changes. Machine learning predicts usage patterns to optimize reserved capacity purchases. Waste elimination is measured and reported as realized savings.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the configuration management database (CMDB) or equivalent for tracking IT assets and their relationships?

rubric.ad_hoc: No CMDB exists. IT asset information is scattered across spreadsheets, ticketing systems, and tribal knowledge. Relationship mapping between assets, services, and business capabilities is not maintained.
rubric.foundational: A CMDB exists but is incomplete and frequently outdated. Manual data entry leads to inaccuracies. Relationship data is limited, undermining impact analysis and change management.
rubric.managed: The CMDB is comprehensive, actively maintained through automated discovery and reconciliation processes. Relationship mapping connects assets to services, applications, and business capabilities. CMDB data supports change management and incident response.
rubric.optimized: A federated CMDB integrates data from multiple discovery and management tools, providing a real-time, accurate view of all IT assets and relationships across hybrid environments. CMDB data drives automated workflows, impact analysis, and service dependency mapping.

items.text: How well does the organization track and manage cloud resource sprawl, including unused instances, unattached storage, and orphaned resources?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud resource sprawl is not tracked. Resources provisioned for development, testing, or temporary purposes persist indefinitely, consuming budget without providing value.
rubric.foundational: Basic cloud resource monitoring identifies some unused resources, but cleanup is ad hoc and incomplete. No automated policies enforce resource lifecycle management.
rubric.managed: Automated cloud resource management identifies and flags unused resources, unattached storage, and orphaned configurations. Cleanup policies are enforced with defined retention periods and automated de-provisioning workflows.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive cloud resource optimization continuously identifies and eliminates waste. Automated lifecycle policies enforce resource hygiene, and anomaly detection flags unexpected resource creation. Cloud waste metrics are tracked and reported as part of FinOps practice.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain accurate, complete, and timely IT asset data across all asset repositories?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset data is unreliable. Multiple conflicting inventories exist, with significant gaps in coverage. Asset data is frequently outdated, leading to poor decision-making.
rubric.foundational: A primary asset repository exists, but data quality is inconsistent. Manual data entry leads to errors, and reconciliation with other data sources is infrequent.
rubric.managed: IT asset data governance includes defined data quality standards, automated discovery to maintain accuracy, regular reconciliation across sources, and assigned data stewards. Data quality metrics are tracked.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive asset data governance ensures continuous data accuracy through automated discovery, reconciliation, and quality monitoring. Machine learning identifies data anomalies. Asset data quality consistently exceeds 95% accuracy across all attributes.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use IT asset data to support financial planning, risk management, and compliance activities?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset data is not used beyond basic inventory purposes. Financial planning, risk management, and compliance activities maintain separate data collections rather than leveraging asset management data.
rubric.foundational: Asset data is occasionally referenced for financial or risk purposes, but data quality and completeness concerns limit its utility. Integration between asset, financial, and risk systems is minimal.
rubric.managed: IT asset data is a trusted data source for financial planning (depreciation, refresh budgeting), risk management (vulnerability assessment, end-of-life tracking), and compliance (license management, FISMA inventory).
rubric.optimized: IT asset data is the authoritative source for multiple business processes. Automated integration feeds asset data to financial, risk, compliance, and service management systems. Asset analytics provide predictive insights for planning and optimization.

items.text: How well does the organization manage IT asset disposal, including data sanitization, environmental compliance, and value recovery?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset disposal is unmanaged. Assets may be stockpiled, improperly disposed of, or discarded without data sanitization. No tracking of disposed assets exists.
rubric.foundational: Basic disposal procedures exist for common asset types, but compliance is inconsistent. Data sanitization may not meet NIST 800-88 standards. Environmental compliance (e.g., e-waste regulations) is not systematically addressed.
rubric.managed: Formal disposal processes cover all asset types with documented data sanitization per NIST 800-88, chain of custody tracking, environmental compliance, and value recovery through resale or trade-in programs.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive disposal management is automated with full chain of custody, certified data sanitization, environmental compliance, and optimized value recovery. Disposal analytics identify opportunities to extend asset life, refurbish for secondary use, or maximize resale value.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage data assets as strategic resources with defined ownership, quality standards, and lifecycle management?

rubric.ad_hoc: Data is not treated as a managed asset. No data ownership, quality standards, or lifecycle management practices exist. Data governance is absent or fragmented across business units.
rubric.foundational: Basic data governance exists for some critical datasets, but ownership is unclear, quality standards are informal, and lifecycle management is not practiced. Data governance is limited to compliance-driven activities.
rubric.managed: Formal data governance program assigns data stewards, defines quality standards, and implements lifecycle management for all significant data assets. Data catalogs enable discovery and understanding of available data resources aligned with CDO Council guidance.
rubric.optimized: Data is managed as a strategic enterprise asset with comprehensive governance, automated quality monitoring, and optimized lifecycle management. Data valuation informs investment decisions, and data sharing is facilitated through published APIs and machine-readable formats per the OPEN Data Act.

items.text: How effectively does the organization integrate IT asset management data with financial management for depreciation, budgeting, and TCO analysis?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT asset and financial data exist in separate systems with no integration. Depreciation schedules, asset valuations, and TCO calculations cannot leverage accurate asset inventory data.
rubric.foundational: Basic integration exists between asset and financial systems for major asset categories, but data reconciliation is manual and gaps exist in coverage.
rubric.managed: IT asset management data is integrated with financial systems, providing accurate asset valuations, automated depreciation calculations, and TCO analysis based on actual asset data. Integration covers all significant asset categories.
rubric.optimized: Seamless asset-financial integration enables real-time asset valuation, predictive depreciation modeling, and comprehensive TCO analysis. Asset data drives budget planning for refresh cycles, maintenance contracts, and end-of-life transitions. Financial analysis supports strategic asset decisions.

items.text: To what extent does the organization use asset utilization data to identify optimization opportunities and reduce waste?

rubric.ad_hoc: Asset utilization is not measured. Over-provisioned, underutilized, or idle assets waste budget without detection.
rubric.foundational: Utilization data is available for some asset types (e.g., server CPU utilization, printer usage) but is not systematically analyzed for optimization opportunities.
rubric.managed: Asset utilization is measured across all major asset categories. Regular analysis identifies underutilized assets for right-sizing, redeployment, or retirement. Optimization actions are tracked with documented savings.
rubric.optimized: Continuous utilization monitoring uses automated analytics to identify optimization opportunities in real time. Machine learning predicts future utilization patterns. A systematic optimization program captures savings from right-sizing, consolidation, and retirement across the entire asset portfolio.

items.text: How well does the organization manage total cost of ownership for the technology asset portfolio, including hidden costs?

rubric.ad_hoc: Asset TCO is not calculated. Decisions about asset acquisition, maintenance, and replacement consider only purchase price, ignoring operational, support, and disposal costs.
rubric.foundational: TCO is estimated for major asset categories using rough calculations, but hidden costs (training, integration, floor space, power, cooling, support labor) are often excluded.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive TCO models cover all significant asset categories, incorporating acquisition, deployment, operation, maintenance, support, and disposal costs. TCO data informs procurement standards and refresh planning.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic TCO models use actual cost data from integrated asset, financial, and operational systems to calculate precise per-asset and per-category costs. TCO trends drive strategic decisions about technology standards, refresh cycles, and delivery model choices (own vs. lease vs. cloud).

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and manage IT assets across geographically distributed locations and remote workforce environments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Asset tracking in distributed locations is unreliable. Remote sites and telework-issued assets may not be inventoried. The organization has limited visibility into the location and condition of dispersed assets.
rubric.foundational: Distributed assets are tracked through periodic manual inventories, but data is frequently outdated. Remote workforce assets (laptops, mobile devices) may not be consistently tracked after deployment.
rubric.managed: Automated discovery and management tools provide visibility into assets across all locations, including remote workforce devices. Mobile device management and endpoint management tools maintain asset records for dispersed hardware.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive distributed asset management provides real-time visibility into all assets regardless of location. Automated compliance checking ensures all deployed assets meet security and configuration standards. Analytics identify location-specific optimization opportunities and manage logistics for refresh and support.

items.text: How effectively does the organization comply with FITARA requirements for CIO authority over IT spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: CIO authority over IT spending is not established per FITARA requirements. IT spending decisions are decentralized with no CIO visibility or approval authority over bureau/component IT budgets.
rubric.foundational: CIO has nominal authority over IT spending as required by FITARA, but in practice, significant IT spending occurs outside CIO purview. Shadow IT spending is prevalent.
rubric.managed: CIO authority over IT spending is established and enforced per FITARA. All IT spending above defined thresholds requires CIO review and approval. IT spending by bureaus/components is visible and governed.
rubric.optimized: Full CIO authority is embedded in organizational culture and processes. IT spending governance is efficient, risk-based, and enables timely decision-making while ensuring appropriate oversight. FITARA scorecard grades reflect strong compliance.

items.text: How well does the organization track and report on FITARA scorecard metrics?

rubric.ad_hoc: FITARA scorecard metrics are not systematically tracked. Scorecard grades are unknown or addressed reactively when congressional attention is drawn.
rubric.foundational: FITARA scorecard categories are understood, and data is gathered for reporting, but tracking is manual and improvement efforts are not coordinated across scorecard areas.
rubric.managed: FITARA scorecard metrics are systematically tracked with assigned owners for each category. Improvement plans exist for underperforming areas with measurable targets and regular progress reporting.
rubric.optimized: FITARA compliance is integrated into IT governance with automated metric tracking, proactive improvement initiatives, and consistent high marks. The organization is a model for FITARA implementation.

items.text: To what extent does the organization implement FITARA requirements for IT acquisition review and approval?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT acquisitions are not subject to CIO review per FITARA. Procurement of IT products and services occurs without centralized visibility or governance.
rubric.foundational: CIO review of IT acquisitions is required by policy, but the review process is inconsistent. Some acquisitions bypass the process due to urgency or classification as non-IT.
rubric.managed: A structured IT acquisition review process ensures CIO involvement in all significant IT procurements. Review criteria, timelines, and escalation paths are defined and followed consistently.
rubric.optimized: IT acquisition review is efficient, risk-based, and adds value beyond compliance. Strategic acquisitions receive early CIO engagement. The process is integrated with strategic sourcing and vendor management.

items.text: How effectively does the organization implement FITARA requirements for CIO authority over IT workforce management, including hiring, training, and performance evaluation?

rubric.ad_hoc: CIO has no role in IT workforce decisions. IT hiring, training, and performance management are handled entirely by individual business units without CIO input or coordination.
rubric.foundational: CIO provides advisory input on IT workforce decisions for some positions, but authority is limited. IT skills gap analysis and workforce planning are not coordinated through the CIO office.
rubric.managed: CIO has formal authority over IT workforce decisions including hiring approval for IT positions, skills gap analysis, and training investment. IT workforce planning is coordinated through the CIO office per FITARA Section 831.
rubric.optimized: Full CIO authority over IT workforce management drives strategic human capital development. Comprehensive IT skills inventories, career development frameworks, and succession plans are maintained. IT workforce metrics are reported to OMB through the FITARA implementation plan.

items.text: How effectively does the organization submit required data to the Federal IT Dashboard (IT Portfolio)?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT Dashboard submissions are late, incomplete, or inaccurate. The organization struggles to compile the required data and treats submissions as a compliance burden.
rubric.foundational: IT Dashboard submissions are made on time but data quality is inconsistent. Investment data may not reflect current status, and risk ratings may not be regularly updated.
rubric.managed: IT Dashboard data is accurate, timely, and complete. Investment information is regularly updated to reflect current status, risk, and cost/schedule performance. Data quality reviews are performed before submission.
rubric.optimized: IT Dashboard management is automated with data feeds from portfolio, financial, and project management systems. The dashboard is used as an active management tool, not just a reporting obligation.

items.text: How well does the organization comply with OMB M-17-22 requirements for IT spending transparency?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization is not aware of or does not comply with M-17-22 requirements for IT spending transparency. IT costs cannot be reported by the required categories.
rubric.foundational: Basic compliance with M-17-22 is achieved, but data quality issues persist. IT costs are reported at a high level but may not fully align with required TBM taxonomy categories.
rubric.managed: The organization fully complies with M-17-22, reporting IT costs using the TBM taxonomy with accurate mapping to cost pools, towers, and services. Data is validated before submission.
rubric.optimized: M-17-22 compliance is seamless, with automated data flows from financial systems to TBM taxonomy reporting. The organization goes beyond minimum requirements, using the data for internal decision-making and optimization.

items.text: How effectively does the organization respond to congressional and oversight body requests for IT spending information?

rubric.ad_hoc: Responses to congressional and GAO requests for IT information are slow, incomplete, and require significant manual effort. Data inconsistencies between responses undermine credibility.
rubric.foundational: The organization can respond to oversight requests but requires significant effort to compile data. Responses may take weeks and involve reconciling data from multiple sources.
rubric.managed: IT spending data is organized to support rapid response to oversight requests. Standard reports can be produced quickly, and ad hoc requests are fulfilled within defined timelines.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive, auditable IT spending data is readily available and can be sliced by any dimension requested. Responses to oversight requests are timely, consistent, and backed by transparent methodology.

items.text: How well does the organization comply with IT spending reporting requirements under the FITARA IT Dashboard and OMB Integrated Data Collection (IDC)?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT spending data reported to OMB is incomplete, inaccurate, or submitted late. The organization struggles to reconcile internal IT spending data with OMB reporting requirements.
rubric.foundational: Basic compliance with IT Dashboard and IDC reporting exists, but data quality issues are common. Manual data gathering and reconciliation processes introduce errors and consume significant staff effort.
rubric.managed: IT spending reporting is accurate, timely, and integrated with internal financial management processes. Automated data feeds from financial systems support OMB reporting requirements, and data quality reviews are performed before each submission.
rubric.optimized: IT spending reporting is exemplary with fully automated, validated data pipelines to OMB systems. The organization proactively identifies and resolves data quality issues, and reporting processes are integrated with continuous financial management.

items.text: How mature is the organization's Capital Planning and Investment Control (CPIC) process for IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal CPIC process exists or the process is perfunctory. IT investments are not evaluated through a structured capital planning framework as required by OMB Circular A-130.
rubric.foundational: A CPIC process exists and meets minimum OMB requirements, but the process is paper-driven and focused on compliance rather than improving investment decisions.
rubric.managed: CPIC is a well-functioning governance process that evaluates IT investments through select, control, and evaluate phases. The process produces useful analysis that genuinely informs investment decisions.
rubric.optimized: CPIC is integrated with TBM, strategic planning, and enterprise architecture. Data-driven analysis supports all phases. The process is agile enough to accommodate different investment types while maintaining rigor.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use Exhibit 300 (Capital Asset Plans) and Exhibit 53 (IT Investment Portfolio) for investment management?

rubric.ad_hoc: Exhibit 300 and 53 submissions are treated as annual compliance exercises with minimal connection to actual investment management. Data may be stale or inconsistent with other reports.
rubric.foundational: Exhibits are submitted on time and meet formatting requirements, but content quality varies. Investment descriptions and performance data may not reflect current reality.
rubric.managed: Exhibit 300 and 53 data accurately reflects investment status, performance, and plans. Data is maintained throughout the year and used for internal portfolio management, not just OMB submission.
rubric.optimized: Exhibit data is automatically generated from integrated portfolio, financial, and project management systems. Real-time investment data supports both OMB reporting and internal decision-making with no duplication of effort.

items.text: How well does the organization integrate TBM data into federal budget formulation and execution processes?

rubric.ad_hoc: TBM data is not used in budget formulation or execution. IT budgets are developed using traditional methods without leveraging cost transparency, unit costs, or benchmarking data.
rubric.foundational: TBM data is available but not systematically integrated into budget processes. Budget analysts may reference TBM reports but rely primarily on traditional budget development methods.
rubric.managed: TBM data is a standard input to IT budget formulation, providing cost transparency, unit costs, and benchmarking context. Budget decisions are informed by TBM analysis.
rubric.optimized: TBM is fully integrated with the budget lifecycle. Cost models, unit economics, and benchmarks drive budget formulation. Real-time TBM data supports budget execution monitoring and mid-year adjustments.

items.text: How effectively does the organization comply with OMB Circular A-130 requirements for IT planning, budgeting, governance, and acquisition?

rubric.ad_hoc: OMB A-130 requirements are not systematically addressed. Compliance is reactive and driven by audit findings rather than proactive governance.
rubric.foundational: Basic awareness of A-130 requirements exists, but implementation is inconsistent across the organization. Some requirements are addressed in policy but not in practice.
rubric.managed: A-130 requirements are systematically implemented through documented policies, processes, and controls. Regular self-assessments verify compliance, and gaps are tracked with remediation plans.
rubric.optimized: Full A-130 compliance is embedded in organizational culture and operations. The organization exceeds baseline requirements and actively contributes to government-wide IT policy development through interagency working groups.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain audit readiness for IT financial management and investment governance?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT financial data and investment documentation are not maintained in an audit-ready state. GAO or IG audit requests trigger scrambles to locate and compile information.
rubric.foundational: Basic documentation exists for major IT investments and financial processes, but audit readiness is inconsistent. Responses to audit requests require significant manual effort.
rubric.managed: IT financial management and investment governance maintain audit-ready documentation as a standard practice. Evidence of compliance with FITARA, CPIC, and financial management policies is systematically maintained.
rubric.optimized: Continuous audit readiness is embedded in IT management processes. Automated evidence collection and documentation ensure rapid response to audits. The organization proactively addresses potential audit findings before they occur.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track and remediate GAO and Inspector General recommendations related to IT management?

rubric.ad_hoc: GAO and IG recommendations are tracked informally, if at all. Remediation plans may not exist or are not monitored for completion.
rubric.foundational: Recommendations are tracked in a basic system, but remediation plans lack specificity, timelines, or accountability. Some recommendations remain open for extended periods without progress.
rubric.managed: A formal tracking system monitors all open recommendations with specific remediation plans, responsible officials, milestones, and target closure dates. Regular status reviews drive timely remediation.
rubric.optimized: Proactive recommendation management closes findings rapidly and uses root cause analysis to prevent recurrence. The organization's IT management maturity results in fewer findings over time. Closed recommendations are validated through independent assessment.

items.text: How well does the organization demonstrate compliance with OMB Circular A-130 requirements for managing federal information resources?

rubric.ad_hoc: Awareness of A-130 requirements for IT resource management is limited. Compliance is not assessed or documented.
rubric.foundational: A-130 requirements are understood at a high level, but compliance is uneven. Some requirements (e.g., information security, records management) receive attention while others (e.g., IT planning, performance measurement) are neglected.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive A-130 compliance is assessed regularly across all requirement areas. Gaps are identified and addressed through documented remediation plans. Compliance evidence is maintained for oversight reporting.
rubric.optimized: A-130 compliance is fully integrated into IT management practice. The organization exceeds minimum requirements and uses A-130 as a framework for management excellence. Compliance is demonstrated through measurable outcomes rather than process documentation alone.

items.text: How well does the organization maintain audit readiness for IT-related financial and operational audits, including IG and GAO reviews?

rubric.ad_hoc: Audit readiness is poor. IT financial records, system documentation, and control evidence are disorganized and incomplete. Audit preparation is a scramble that diverts resources from operations.
rubric.foundational: Basic audit preparation processes exist, but audit readiness is inconsistent. Some documentation and evidence are maintained, but gaps are common and require significant effort to address during audit engagements.
rubric.managed: Continuous audit readiness is maintained for IT financial and operational domains. Documentation, evidence, and control testing results are organized and accessible. Audit liaison processes are well-defined and efficient.
rubric.optimized: Audit readiness is fully embedded in IT operations. Automated control monitoring and evidence collection ensure continuous compliance. Audit engagements are routine and efficient, with minimal operational disruption. The organization proactively addresses emerging audit focus areas.

items.text: How effectively does the organization comply with the Digital Accountability and Transparency Act (DATA Act) requirements for IT spending data?

rubric.ad_hoc: DATA Act compliance for IT spending is minimal. IT spending data submitted to USASpending.gov is inaccurate or incomplete. No process connects IT financial management to DATA Act reporting.
rubric.foundational: Basic DATA Act compliance exists, but IT spending data quality issues persist. Reconciliation between internal IT financial data and DATA Act submissions is not performed.
rubric.managed: IT spending data is accurately reported under the DATA Act with documented processes connecting IT financial management to USASpending.gov submissions. Regular reconciliation ensures data quality.
rubric.optimized: DATA Act reporting is fully automated and integrated with TBM financial management. IT spending data in USASpending.gov is accurate, timely, and used by the organization as a transparency tool. The organization's IT spending data consistently receives high quality scores from IG assessments.

items.text: To what extent does the organization provide proactive IT spending transparency to congressional appropriators, oversight bodies, and the public?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT spending transparency is limited to required reports. No proactive effort is made to communicate IT investment strategies, costs, or outcomes to external stakeholders.
rubric.foundational: Some proactive communication occurs (e.g., CIO testimony, annual reports), but IT cost data shared externally is high-level and not connected to outcomes or benchmarks.
rubric.managed: The organization proactively shares IT spending data with appropriators and oversight bodies, including unit costs, benchmarks, and investment outcomes. Public-facing dashboards provide transparency into IT spending.
rubric.optimized: IT spending transparency is a strategic priority. The organization provides comprehensive, accessible spending data to all stakeholders, demonstrates value for taxpayer dollars through outcome-based reporting, and is recognized as a transparency leader across government.

items.text: How well does the organization use IT spending transparency to build stakeholder confidence and support for IT investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: IT spending opacity erodes stakeholder confidence. Budget requests are viewed skeptically because the organization cannot demonstrate efficient use of current funding.
rubric.foundational: Basic cost information is shared with stakeholders, but the connection between spending and outcomes is weak. Stakeholders remain uncertain about IT investment value.
rubric.managed: IT cost transparency actively builds stakeholder confidence by demonstrating cost discipline, benchmark performance, and investment outcomes. Transparency data supports budget requests and investment proposals.
rubric.optimized: IT spending transparency is a competitive advantage in securing resources. The organization's demonstrated track record of cost management, supported by comprehensive TBM data, gives stakeholders confidence in IT stewardship and facilitates approval of strategic investment requests.

items.text: How well does the organization leverage Digital Accountability and Transparency Act (DATA Act) reporting data to improve IT spending transparency and decision-making?

rubric.ad_hoc: DATA Act reporting is treated as a compliance exercise with no connection to IT spending transparency or decision-making. IT spending data in DATA Act submissions is not reconciled with internal reporting.
rubric.foundational: DATA Act submissions include IT spending data, but it is not used for internal analysis or decision-making. Reconciliation between DATA Act and internal IT spending data is performed only for audit purposes.
rubric.managed: DATA Act reporting data is reconciled with internal IT spending data and used to enhance spending transparency. Insights from DATA Act data (e.g., award-level spending patterns) supplement internal cost analysis.
rubric.optimized: DATA Act data is fully integrated into the IT spending transparency ecosystem. Award-level, sub-award, and obligation data enhance internal cost analysis and benchmarking. The organization uses DATA Act data to identify optimization opportunities and validate vendor spending.

items.text: How well-defined is the organization's strategy for IT shared services?

rubric.ad_hoc: No shared services strategy exists. IT services are duplicated across organizational units without consideration of consolidation or shared delivery models.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that shared services could reduce costs and improve quality, but no formal strategy or roadmap has been developed. Sharing occurs organically and inconsistently.
rubric.managed: A documented shared services strategy identifies candidate services, target operating models, and a phased implementation roadmap. Business cases quantify expected benefits of each consolidation initiative.
rubric.optimized: The shared services strategy is integrated with enterprise IT strategy and continuously updated based on actual outcomes and evolving needs. The organization is a model for efficient shared service delivery.

items.text: How effectively does the organization evaluate make-vs-buy-vs-share decisions for IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: No structured analysis informs make, buy, or share decisions. Each organizational unit procures or builds IT capabilities independently without considering shared alternatives.
rubric.foundational: High-level analysis is performed for major decisions, but evaluation criteria are not standardized. Available shared services (internal or government-wide, such as GSA or shared service providers) may not be considered.
rubric.managed: A structured decision framework evaluates make, buy, and share options for all significant IT services. Government-wide shared services (e.g., QSMO offerings) are systematically evaluated as options.
rubric.optimized: Decision frameworks incorporate total cost analysis, quality benchmarks, strategic fit, and risk assessment. The organization actively monitors emerging shared service offerings and continuously optimizes its delivery model mix.

items.text: To what extent does the organization leverage government-wide shared services and Quality Service Management Offices (QSMOs)?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization is not aware of or does not utilize government-wide shared services or QSMO offerings. All IT services are procured or built independently.
rubric.foundational: Some government-wide shared services are used (e.g., GSA contracts, shared email) but adoption is limited and not part of a strategic approach.
rubric.managed: The organization systematically evaluates QSMO offerings and government-wide shared services for applicable functions. Adoption decisions are documented with business case support.
rubric.optimized: The organization is an active participant in shared services ecosystems, both consuming and potentially providing services. Continuous monitoring of QSMO and shared service offerings informs service delivery decisions.

items.text: How well does the organization align its shared services strategy with government-wide shared services initiatives and OMB designated providers?

rubric.ad_hoc: No alignment with government-wide shared services initiatives exists. The organization operates independently without considering OMB-designated shared service providers or Quality Service Management Offices (QSMOs).
rubric.foundational: Awareness of government-wide shared services initiatives exists, but the organization has not assessed its services against QSMO offerings or OMB marketplace designations.
rubric.managed: The organization has assessed its services against QSMO offerings and OMB shared services marketplace. Migration plans exist for services where designated providers offer better value. The organization actively participates in QSMO governance forums.
rubric.optimized: Full alignment with government-wide shared services strategy. The organization serves as an early adopter of QSMO services, contributes to standards development, and uses shared services to achieve cost savings and mission focus per PMA Cross-Agency Priority goals.

items.text: How effectively has the organization consolidated redundant IT services and infrastructure across organizational units?

rubric.ad_hoc: Significant redundancy exists across organizational units (e.g., multiple help desks, duplicate enterprise applications, separate network operations). No consolidation efforts are underway.
rubric.foundational: Some consolidation has occurred for infrastructure (e.g., data center consolidation) but application and service consolidation is limited. Redundancies persist due to organizational resistance or technical complexity.
rubric.managed: A structured consolidation program addresses redundancies across infrastructure, applications, and services. Progress is tracked against a roadmap with measured cost savings and quality improvements.
rubric.optimized: Continuous portfolio optimization identifies and eliminates redundancies as they emerge. Consolidation is culturally accepted and enabled by standardized platforms and APIs. The organization achieves high efficiency with minimal duplication.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the financial aspects of shared service transitions (cost allocation, pricing, funding)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Financial aspects of shared services are not managed. Costs for shared services are absorbed by the providing organization without cost recovery or transparent pricing.
rubric.foundational: Basic cost allocation exists for shared services, but pricing models are simple (e.g., equal split) and may not reflect actual consumption or service differentiation.
rubric.managed: Shared services have transparent pricing models based on consumption or tiered service levels. Funding mechanisms (working capital funds, fee-for-service, or chargeback) are established and operational.
rubric.optimized: Shared service financial models are mature, with consumption-based pricing, competitive benchmarking against alternatives, and continuous optimization. Financial performance is tracked and reported with full transparency.

items.text: How effectively does the organization maintain service quality and responsiveness during and after consolidation transitions?

rubric.ad_hoc: Consolidation efforts frequently degrade service quality. Customer satisfaction drops significantly during transitions, and post-consolidation service levels may be worse than pre-consolidation.
rubric.foundational: Basic transition planning addresses service continuity, but disruptions still occur. Post-consolidation service levels are eventually restored but may take months to stabilize.
rubric.managed: Structured transition management ensures service quality is maintained throughout consolidation. SLAs are defined for the transition period, and post-consolidation service levels meet or exceed pre-consolidation baselines.
rubric.optimized: Consolidation transitions are executed with minimal disruption using proven methodologies. Post-consolidation service quality consistently improves. Customer satisfaction is measured and targeted throughout the transition lifecycle.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and realize cost savings from IT service consolidation and shared service adoption?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cost savings from consolidation are not measured. Shared services are adopted based on mandates rather than business case analysis, and actual savings are not tracked.
rubric.foundational: Estimated savings from consolidation initiatives are included in business cases, but actual savings realization is not tracked. It is unclear whether shared service adoption achieves projected cost benefits.
rubric.managed: Cost savings from consolidation are formally tracked through defined baseline comparisons and savings realization reports. Actual vs. projected savings are reported to leadership, and shortfalls trigger corrective action.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive savings tracking covers direct cost reductions, avoided costs, and productivity gains from consolidation. Savings are verified through independent validation and reinvested in modernization. Savings data informs future consolidation business cases and improves estimation accuracy.

items.text: How well does the organization govern shared IT services, including decision rights, service levels, and dispute resolution?

rubric.ad_hoc: No governance structure exists for shared services. Decisions about shared service priorities, funding, and service levels are made unilaterally by the providing organization.
rubric.foundational: Basic governance exists (e.g., a steering committee) but meets infrequently and has limited authority. Service consumers have minimal input into shared service decisions.
rubric.managed: A formal governance structure includes representatives from service providers and consumers. Decision rights, funding models, service levels, and escalation paths are clearly defined and followed.
rubric.optimized: Collaborative governance drives continuous improvement with balanced representation, data-driven decision-making, and proactive issue resolution. Governance adapts to evolving needs and stakeholder feedback.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and report on the performance and value of shared IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: Shared service performance is not measured or reported. Neither providers nor consumers have visibility into whether shared services are delivering expected value.
rubric.foundational: Basic performance metrics exist for some shared services, but reporting is inconsistent and does not include value realization (cost savings, quality improvements).
rubric.managed: Comprehensive performance dashboards track shared service quality, cost, customer satisfaction, and value realization. Reports are shared with governance bodies and service consumers regularly.
rubric.optimized: Real-time performance monitoring with benchmarking against internal delivery and external market alternatives. Value realization is continuously tracked and validated. Analytics drive proactive service improvement.

items.text: How well does the organization manage service-level expectations and accountability between shared service providers and consuming business units?

rubric.ad_hoc: No formal accountability framework exists between shared service providers and consumers. Service expectations are undefined, and disputes are resolved through ad hoc escalation.
rubric.foundational: Basic service expectations are communicated informally, but no formal SLAs or accountability mechanisms exist. Consumer satisfaction with shared services is not systematically measured.
rubric.managed: Formal SLAs define service expectations, performance metrics, and escalation procedures between shared service providers and consumers. Regular service reviews with consumer representatives drive continuous improvement.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive governance model includes formal SLAs, financial transparency, consumer advisory boards, and market-competitive benchmarking. Shared services operate with utility-like accountability, and consumers have meaningful voice in service evolution and investment priorities.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and report shared service performance against defined service levels?

rubric.ad_hoc: Shared service performance is not measured. Consuming agencies have no visibility into whether shared services meet expectations, and providers lack accountability metrics.
rubric.foundational: Basic performance metrics are tracked for some shared services, but service level agreements (SLAs) may not be defined, or compliance is not regularly reported to consumers.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive SLAs are defined for all shared services, with regular performance reporting to consumers. Service level compliance is measured, reported monthly, and triggers remediation when targets are missed.
rubric.optimized: Advanced shared service performance management includes real-time dashboards, predictive SLA management, and continuous improvement programs. Consumer satisfaction surveys complement quantitative metrics. Performance data drives service investment decisions.

items.text: To what extent does the organization benchmark shared service costs and quality against market alternatives?

rubric.ad_hoc: Shared service costs are not benchmarked. Consuming agencies cannot determine whether shared services are cost-competitive with commercial or in-house alternatives.
rubric.foundational: Occasional comparisons with market alternatives occur, but methodologies are inconsistent and may not account for all relevant costs (transition, management overhead, compliance).
rubric.managed: Regular benchmarking compares shared service unit costs and quality against commercial alternatives, in-house delivery, and other government shared service providers. Results inform pricing and improvement priorities.
rubric.optimized: Continuous benchmarking drives shared service competitiveness. Provider organizations use benchmark data to set improvement targets and justify investments. Consuming agencies can make informed sourcing decisions based on transparent cost-quality comparisons.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the customer experience and feedback loop for shared IT services?

rubric.ad_hoc: No customer experience management exists for shared services. Consumer complaints are handled reactively, and systematic feedback is not collected or acted upon.
rubric.foundational: Periodic satisfaction surveys are conducted, but response rates are low and results are not systematically used to drive improvements. Complaint resolution processes are informal.
rubric.managed: A structured customer experience program includes regular satisfaction measurement, formal complaint and escalation processes, and service improvement plans based on consumer feedback.
rubric.optimized: Customer experience excellence drives shared service management. Real-time feedback mechanisms, journey mapping, and co-design processes with consumers ensure services evolve to meet changing needs. Net promoter scores and customer effort scores are tracked and improving.

items.text: How effectively does the organization benchmark its shared service costs and quality against commercial alternatives and other government providers?

rubric.ad_hoc: No benchmarking of shared services against alternatives is performed. The organization has no basis for assessing whether its shared services are cost-competitive or quality-competitive.
rubric.foundational: Informal comparisons to commercial alternatives occur during procurement events, but systematic benchmarking of shared service cost and quality is not performed.
rubric.managed: Shared service costs and quality are periodically benchmarked against commercial alternatives and peer government providers. Benchmarking results inform service improvement priorities and make-vs-buy decisions.
rubric.optimized: Continuous benchmarking of shared services against multiple reference points (commercial, government peers, QSMOs) drives competitive service delivery. Benchmark data is transparently shared with consumers and informs strategic service direction.

items.text: How well-defined is the organization's cloud strategy, aligned with the Cloud Smart policy?

rubric.ad_hoc: No cloud strategy exists. Cloud adoption is happening ad hoc, driven by individual projects or teams without a coherent organizational approach.
rubric.foundational: A high-level cloud strategy exists but lacks specifics on workload criteria, migration priorities, security requirements, or financial implications. Alignment with Cloud Smart is superficial.
rubric.managed: A comprehensive cloud strategy addresses security, procurement, and workforce pillars of Cloud Smart. The strategy includes workload assessment criteria, migration roadmaps, and financial models.
rubric.optimized: The cloud strategy is a living document, continuously updated based on outcomes, market evolution, and technology advances. It drives proactive optimization and enables rapid adoption of new cloud capabilities.

items.text: How effectively does the organization evaluate workloads for cloud suitability and select appropriate cloud deployment models?

rubric.ad_hoc: No systematic workload assessment exists. Cloud decisions are made project-by-project based on team preference or vendor influence rather than structured analysis.
rubric.foundational: Basic workload assessment is performed for some applications, considering factors like data sensitivity and technical compatibility. Assessment criteria are not standardized.
rubric.managed: A structured workload assessment framework evaluates all applications for cloud suitability using consistent criteria (6 R's: rehost, replatform, refactor, repurchase, retain, retire). Results inform migration planning.
rubric.optimized: Automated portfolio assessment continuously evaluates workloads against evolving cloud capabilities. Dynamic placement optimization recommends ideal hosting models considering cost, performance, security, and compliance.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the financial planning and investment aspects of cloud migration and modernization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud migration costs are not planned or tracked. The organization is surprised by migration complexity, transition costs, and ongoing cloud operating expenses.
rubric.foundational: Basic cost estimates are prepared for major cloud migrations, but estimates often underestimate transition costs, training, and organizational change management. Total cost of migration is not well understood.
rubric.managed: Cloud migration financial planning includes comprehensive cost modeling (migration costs, transition period dual-running costs, ongoing operating costs, decommission savings). Business cases compare cloud vs. on-premises TCO.
rubric.optimized: Sophisticated financial models incorporate dynamic pricing, reserved capacity optimization, and application-specific cost profiles. Financial outcomes of past migrations inform future estimates. Migration ROI is tracked and validated.

items.text: How well does the organization's cloud strategy incorporate multi-cloud or hybrid-cloud approaches to avoid vendor lock-in and optimize for workload requirements?

rubric.ad_hoc: The organization has no multi-cloud or hybrid strategy. Cloud adoption is limited to a single provider or done ad hoc without strategic consideration of workload placement.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of multi-cloud benefits exists, but the organization defaults to a single cloud provider. Hybrid integration between cloud and on-premises environments is limited and technically challenging.
rubric.managed: A formal multi-cloud or hybrid strategy defines workload placement criteria based on requirements (security, performance, cost, data sovereignty). Integration patterns between cloud providers and on-premises environments are documented and tested.
rubric.optimized: Mature multi-cloud management with automated workload placement optimization, consistent governance across environments, and seamless hybrid integration. The organization leverages provider competition to negotiate optimal pricing and service terms aligned with FedRAMP and Cloud Smart strategy.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and optimize data center utilization and efficiency metrics?

rubric.ad_hoc: Data center utilization and efficiency are not measured. The organization does not track PUE, server utilization, or other data center metrics.
rubric.foundational: Basic metrics (e.g., PUE, rack utilization) are tracked for major data centers, but are not used to drive optimization decisions. Metrics may not be current.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive data center metrics are tracked and reported regularly, including PUE, server utilization, storage utilization, and virtualization ratios. Optimization targets are set and progress monitored.
rubric.optimized: Real-time data center monitoring with automated optimization of power, cooling, and compute resources. Advanced analytics predict capacity needs and optimize workload placement for maximum efficiency.

items.text: How well does the organization execute data center consolidation and closure in alignment with federal mandates?

rubric.ad_hoc: No data center consolidation plan exists. The organization operates more data centers or server rooms than needed, with significant duplication and underutilization.
rubric.foundational: A consolidation plan exists, driven by federal mandates, but execution is slow. Closures are behind schedule, and migration to consolidated facilities or cloud encounters frequent delays.
rubric.managed: Data center consolidation is progressing according to plan with defined milestones, migration schedules, and tracked savings. Closures are executed on schedule with managed risk.
rubric.optimized: Data center optimization is a continuous program. The organization proactively identifies consolidation opportunities beyond federal mandates. Closed facilities realize planned savings, and lessons learned accelerate future closures.

items.text: To what extent does the organization have a comprehensive strategy for managing hybrid environments (on-premises + cloud)?

rubric.ad_hoc: On-premises and cloud environments are managed separately with no integrated strategy. Networking, security, and management tools differ between environments.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of hybrid management challenges exists, but tooling and processes are not unified. Management of cloud and on-premises requires separate skills, tools, and processes.
rubric.managed: A hybrid management strategy defines consistent approaches to security, networking, monitoring, and cost management across on-premises and cloud. Unified tooling provides cross-environment visibility.
rubric.optimized: Seamless hybrid management enables transparent workload mobility between environments. Unified governance, security, and cost management operate consistently regardless of hosting model.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and report data center optimization metrics required by DCOI, including PUE, virtualization, and server utilization?

rubric.ad_hoc: DCOI metrics are not systematically tracked. Data center optimization reporting is manually compiled and submitted reactively to meet compliance deadlines.
rubric.foundational: Basic DCOI metrics are collected, but data quality is inconsistent. PUE calculations may not follow OMB methodology, and server utilization data is estimated rather than measured.
rubric.managed: DCOI metrics are accurately tracked using automated measurement tools. PUE, virtualization ratios, and server utilization are monitored continuously and reported per OMB guidance. Optimization actions are linked to metric improvements.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive DCOI compliance with industry-leading optimization metrics. Automated monitoring drives real-time optimization actions. The organization exceeds OMB targets and uses optimization data to inform cloud migration cost-benefit analyses.

items.text: How effectively does the organization prioritize and plan application modernization investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: No application modernization roadmap exists. Legacy applications are maintained indefinitely without evaluation of modernization options or business impact.
rubric.foundational: Some legacy applications have been identified for modernization, but prioritization is based on crisis (e.g., end of vendor support) rather than strategic value assessment.
rubric.managed: A structured application modernization roadmap prioritizes investments based on business value, technical debt, risk, and modernization cost. Multiple modernization approaches are considered for each application.
rubric.optimized: Continuous application portfolio assessment identifies modernization candidates proactively. Modernization is funded as a strategic program with measurable business outcomes. The organization maintains modern technology currency across its portfolio.

items.text: How well does the organization track and manage the financial aspects of application modernization initiatives?

rubric.ad_hoc: Modernization project costs are not tracked separately from other IT spending. The total investment in modernization and the resulting savings or value creation are unknown.
rubric.foundational: Modernization project budgets are tracked, but lifecycle costs (including transition, parallel running, training, and decommission of legacy systems) are not comprehensively captured.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive financial tracking covers all modernization costs and benefits, including development, migration, transition, decommission, and ongoing operating cost changes. ROI is calculated for each initiative.
rubric.optimized: Portfolio-level modernization financial management optimizes the pace and sequencing of investments. Value realization is tracked continuously, and financial outcomes inform future modernization strategy and business cases.

items.text: To what extent does the organization consider and plan for the workforce impacts of modernization and cloud adoption?

rubric.ad_hoc: Workforce impacts of modernization are not considered until after technology changes are implemented, resulting in skill gaps, resistance, and delayed value realization.
rubric.foundational: Basic awareness of workforce impact exists, and some training is provided for new technologies, but workforce planning is not integrated with modernization planning.
rubric.managed: Workforce impact assessments are a standard part of modernization planning. Training programs, hiring plans, and organizational change management are included in modernization budgets and timelines.
rubric.optimized: Proactive workforce transformation programs continuously develop cloud-native and modern technology skills. The organization attracts and retains modern technology talent and manages the transition from legacy skills strategically.

items.text: How well does the organization apply the appropriate cloud migration strategy (rehost, replatform, refactor, retire, retain) for each application based on objective analysis?

rubric.ad_hoc: No structured cloud migration decision framework exists. Migration decisions are made ad hoc based on technical preference or vendor marketing rather than objective workload analysis.
rubric.foundational: A basic migration decision framework exists, but application assessments are superficial. Most migrations default to rehosting without evaluating whether replatforming or refactoring would deliver greater value.
rubric.managed: A structured 6-R assessment framework is applied to all migration candidates, evaluating technical complexity, business value, cost implications, and security requirements. Migration strategies are tailored to each application and documented in migration plans.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive, data-driven migration analysis uses automated assessment tools, application dependency mapping, and financial modeling to determine optimal migration strategies. Post-migration reviews validate strategy selection and inform future assessments.

items.text: How effectively does the organization manage application modernization to leverage cloud-native capabilities (containers, serverless, managed services) for improved agility and cost efficiency?

rubric.ad_hoc: Application modernization to leverage cloud-native capabilities is not pursued. Migrated applications run as-is in cloud environments without taking advantage of cloud-native services.
rubric.foundational: Some applications have been partially modernized (e.g., containerized), but cloud-native adoption is limited to pilot projects. Development teams lack skills and tools for cloud-native development.
rubric.managed: A cloud-native modernization program systematically evaluates applications for containerization, serverless adoption, and managed service utilization. Development teams are trained in cloud-native practices, and a platform engineering function supports adoption.
rubric.optimized: Cloud-native development is the standard for new applications and actively pursued for existing systems. A mature platform engineering practice provides self-service development environments, CI/CD pipelines, and observability. Cloud-native adoption metrics demonstrate measurable improvements in deployment velocity and operational cost.

items.text: How effectively does the organization plan and budget for Zero Trust Architecture implementation as part of IT modernization per OMB M-22-09?

rubric.ad_hoc: Zero Trust is recognized as a requirement but has no dedicated budget or investment plan. Implementation is unfunded or relies on redirecting existing security spending without strategic planning.
rubric.foundational: A high-level Zero Trust implementation plan exists with rough cost estimates, but detailed financial planning across the five pillars (identity, devices, networks, applications, data) is incomplete.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive Zero Trust investment planning covers all five pillars with phased implementation budgets, milestone-based funding, and clear alignment with OMB M-22-09 timelines. Spending is tracked against plan.
rubric.optimized: Zero Trust investment is fully integrated with enterprise modernization planning and TBM cost management. Financial models project multi-year costs including technology, workforce, and process changes. ROI analysis quantifies risk reduction value alongside implementation costs.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track and optimize spending on application and infrastructure modernization under the Cloud Smart strategy?

rubric.ad_hoc: Modernization spending is not tracked separately from general IT spending. The organization cannot quantify its investment in modernization versus sustaining legacy systems.
rubric.foundational: Modernization spending is identified at a high level (e.g., total cloud migration budget), but detailed tracking by workload, migration approach (rehost, replatform, refactor), and phase is not available.
rubric.managed: Modernization spending is tracked by workload, migration approach, and phase. Unit costs per migration are calculated and compared across approaches. Spending is aligned with Cloud Smart strategy priorities.
rubric.optimized: Advanced modernization financial management includes predictive cost modeling, automated tracking, and optimization of migration approach selection based on cost-benefit analysis. The organization demonstrates measurable cost savings and capability improvements from modernization investments.

items.text: How well does the organization manage the financial aspects of technical debt reduction as part of modernization?

rubric.ad_hoc: Technical debt is not quantified financially. The cost of maintaining legacy systems, workarounds, and deferred maintenance is unknown and not factored into modernization business cases.
rubric.foundational: Technical debt is acknowledged qualitatively, and rough estimates exist for the cost of major legacy systems. However, technical debt is not systematically quantified or tracked.
rubric.managed: Technical debt is quantified using defined methodologies (e.g., maintenance cost premium, opportunity cost of delayed modernization). Technical debt reduction targets are set and tracked as part of the modernization program.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive technical debt management quantifies debt across the portfolio, prioritizes reduction based on cost-benefit analysis, and tracks reduction over time. Technical debt metrics are reported to leadership and inform investment prioritization. The organization demonstrates declining technical debt ratios.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure and communicate the business value delivered by modernization investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Modernization value is not measured. Investments are justified based on technical necessity (e.g., end of support) rather than business value. Post-modernization benefits are not tracked.
rubric.foundational: Business value is described in modernization business cases, but specific metrics are not defined and post-implementation value measurement is rare.
rubric.managed: Defined value metrics (cost savings, performance improvement, user satisfaction, mission capability) are tracked for all significant modernization investments. Value realization reports are shared with leadership.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive value management tracks the full business impact of modernization, including cost reduction, service improvement, agility gains, risk reduction, and mission capability enhancement. Value data builds the case for continued modernization investment and informs strategy adjustments.

items.text: How well does the organization align cloud security architecture with the federal Zero Trust maturity model and CISA guidance?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cloud security architecture does not incorporate Zero Trust principles. Traditional perimeter-based security models are applied to cloud environments without adaptation.
rubric.foundational: Some Zero Trust concepts are acknowledged in cloud security design, but implementation is limited to basic identity verification. CISA Zero Trust maturity model guidance has not been systematically assessed against current capabilities.
rubric.managed: Cloud security architecture is aligned with CISA Zero Trust maturity model across identity, device, network, application, and data pillars. A phased implementation roadmap addresses gaps, and progress is tracked against OMB M-22-09 milestones.
rubric.optimized: Advanced Zero Trust implementation in cloud environments with continuous verification, micro-segmentation, encrypted traffic inspection, and data-centric security. The organization achieves optimal maturity across CISA pillars and serves as a model for peer agencies.

items.text: How effectively does the organization track and report total cybersecurity spending across all funding sources?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity spending is not separately tracked. Security costs are embedded in infrastructure, application, and personnel budgets with no ability to determine total cybersecurity investment.
rubric.foundational: Some cybersecurity spending is tracked (e.g., dedicated security tools, security team personnel) but embedded security costs (security features in infrastructure, developer time on security) are not captured.
rubric.managed: Total cybersecurity spending is comprehensively tracked across all categories: dedicated security tools, personnel, embedded security costs, compliance activities, and incident response. Spending is mapped to NIST CSF functions.
rubric.optimized: Real-time cybersecurity spend visibility provides granular views by NIST CSF function, attack surface area, asset type, and business unit. Spending trends are analyzed and benchmarked against peers and industry standards.

items.text: How well does the organization map cybersecurity investments to the NIST Cybersecurity Framework functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity investments are not mapped to any framework. The organization cannot determine how much it invests in preventive vs. detective vs. responsive capabilities.
rubric.foundational: A rough mapping of major cybersecurity tools and programs to NIST CSF functions exists, but it is incomplete and not used for investment decision-making.
rubric.managed: All cybersecurity investments are mapped to NIST CSF functions and categories, enabling analysis of investment balance across the framework. Gaps in coverage are identified and addressed.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic mapping of investments to NIST CSF is automated and continuously updated. Investment balance is optimized based on risk assessment, threat intelligence, and maturity targets for each function.

items.text: To what extent does the organization report cybersecurity spending in compliance with federal requirements (e.g., OMB reporting, CDM program)?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity spending is not reported per federal requirements. Compliance with OMB reporting requirements for cybersecurity investment is incomplete or inaccurate.
rubric.foundational: Basic cybersecurity spending data is compiled for OMB reporting, but data quality is inconsistent. CDM program costs and benefits are not clearly tracked.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity spending is accurately reported per federal requirements, with clear documentation of methodology. CDM program investments and outcomes are tracked and reported.
rubric.optimized: Automated cybersecurity spending reporting meets all federal requirements with high data quality. The organization goes beyond compliance to use reported data for internal optimization and strategic planning.

items.text: How well does the organization track cybersecurity spending as a percentage of total IT spending and benchmark it against federal averages and NIST framework coverage?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity spending is not separately tracked from overall IT spending. The organization cannot report its cybersecurity investment level or compare it to benchmarks.
rubric.foundational: Basic cybersecurity spending is tracked at a high level, but the total is estimated and may not capture all security-related costs (e.g., embedded security in application development, personnel training).
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity spending is comprehensively tracked, including direct and embedded security costs. Spending is benchmarked against federal averages and analyzed by NIST CSF function (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover).
rubric.optimized: Cybersecurity investment analytics provide real-time visibility into spending by NIST CSF function, threat category, and asset class. Investment levels are continuously benchmarked and optimized to maximize risk reduction per dollar invested.

items.text: How effectively does the organization allocate cybersecurity investments across prevention, detection, and response capabilities based on threat landscape analysis?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity investment allocation is not informed by threat analysis. Spending is concentrated on compliance-driven controls without assessing whether the investment mix addresses the most significant threats.
rubric.foundational: High-level threat awareness influences some investment decisions, but allocation across prevention, detection, and response is not strategically balanced. Compliance-driven spending dominates over risk-informed investment.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity investment allocation is informed by threat landscape analysis, vulnerability assessments, and risk prioritization. A balanced portfolio funds prevention, detection, and response capabilities proportional to assessed risk.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic, threat-informed cybersecurity investment optimization continuously adjusts allocation based on real-time threat intelligence, incident data, and attack surface changes. Investment effectiveness is measured through metrics like mean time to detect and respond.

items.text: How effectively does the organization use risk assessment to prioritize cybersecurity investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity investment decisions are reactive, driven by the latest breach headlines, audit findings, or vendor marketing rather than systematic risk assessment.
rubric.foundational: Risk assessments are performed periodically, but the connection between risk findings and investment prioritization is loose. Investment decisions may not address the highest-priority risks.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity investments are prioritized based on formal risk assessment results, considering threat likelihood, vulnerability exposure, and business impact. Investment proposals include risk reduction quantification.
rubric.optimized: Continuous risk-informed investment optimization uses real-time threat intelligence, vulnerability data, and business impact analysis. Investment portfolio is dynamically adjusted to address evolving risk landscape.

items.text: To what extent does the organization quantify the expected risk reduction or business value of cybersecurity investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity investment benefits are described qualitatively (e.g., 'improves security posture') without any attempt to quantify risk reduction or business value.
rubric.foundational: Some quantification is attempted for major investments using basic methods, but approaches are inconsistent and may not be credible to business stakeholders or investment review boards.
rubric.managed: Standardized methodologies (e.g., FAIR, annualized loss expectancy) quantify expected risk reduction for cybersecurity investments. Business cases include quantified risk-benefit analysis.
rubric.optimized: Sophisticated risk quantification models, validated against actual incident data, inform all cybersecurity investment decisions. Risk reduction ROI is calculated and tracked, enabling optimization of the cybersecurity portfolio.
