items.text: How well does the organization balance cybersecurity investment across preventive, detective, and responsive capabilities?

rubric.ad_hoc: Investment balance across security capabilities is not considered. Spending is heavily concentrated in one area (typically preventive tools) with gaps in others.
rubric.foundational: Awareness exists that balanced investment is important, but no systematic analysis ensures appropriate distribution across preventive, detective, and responsive capabilities.
rubric.managed: Investment analysis evaluates the balance across prevention, detection, and response. Gaps are identified through capability maturity assessment and addressed through targeted investment.
rubric.optimized: Dynamic investment balancing uses threat modeling, attack simulation results, and maturity assessments to optimize the distribution of spending across all security capability areas.

items.text: How well does the organization quantify cyber risk in financial terms to support investment decision-making and communicate risk exposure to leadership?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cyber risk is described qualitatively (high/medium/low) without financial quantification. Leadership cannot assess whether cybersecurity investments are proportional to actual risk exposure.
rubric.foundational: Some cyber risk quantification is attempted for major threat scenarios, but methodologies are informal and results are not consistently used in investment decisions.
rubric.managed: Cyber risk is quantified using established methodologies (e.g., FAIR framework) for priority threat scenarios. Financial risk estimates inform cybersecurity budget requests and are communicated to leadership in business terms.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive cyber risk quantification covers all significant threat scenarios with probabilistic financial impact models. Risk quantification is automated, continuously updated with threat intelligence, and directly linked to investment prioritization and insurance decisions.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure the return on cybersecurity investments?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity ROI is not measured. The organization cannot articulate whether its cybersecurity spending is effective or whether additional investment would be justified.
rubric.foundational: Basic effectiveness metrics exist (e.g., number of blocked attacks, patch compliance rates) but are not connected to investment levels or used to evaluate ROI.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity ROI is evaluated using defined metrics linking investment levels to outcomes (e.g., risk reduction per dollar, cost per incident prevented, mean time to detect/respond improvements).
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive cybersecurity ROI measurement integrates financial investment data with security outcome metrics, risk quantification, and business impact analysis. Results drive continuous portfolio optimization.

items.text: To what extent does the organization consolidate and optimize its cybersecurity tool portfolio to maximize value?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity tools have been acquired incrementally without portfolio-level assessment. Significant overlap, redundancy, and underutilization exist across the security tool landscape.
rubric.foundational: Awareness of tool sprawl exists, and some consolidation has occurred, but the full extent of overlap and underutilization is not well-understood.
rubric.managed: Regular security tool portfolio assessments evaluate utilization, overlap, integration, and effectiveness. A consolidation and optimization roadmap reduces redundancy and improves value from existing investments.
rubric.optimized: Continuous security tool portfolio management optimizes the technology stack using utilization analytics, capability gap analysis, and total cost of ownership evaluation. Tool rationalization is a routine practice.

items.text: How well does the organization communicate cybersecurity investment effectiveness to executive leadership and stakeholders?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity effectiveness is not communicated to leadership in business terms. Security briefings focus on technical threats and incidents without connecting to investment decisions or business impact.
rubric.foundational: Periodic cybersecurity reports are provided to leadership, but content is primarily technical. Business stakeholders struggle to understand the value of cybersecurity investments.
rubric.managed: Executive cybersecurity reporting translates security metrics into business terms, showing investment effectiveness, risk reduction trends, and benchmarking against peers. Reports inform budget and investment decisions.
rubric.optimized: Real-time executive dashboards provide business-contextualized cybersecurity investment visibility. Board-level reporting connects cybersecurity investment to enterprise risk management and strategic objectives.

items.text: How effectively does the organization measure the return on cybersecurity investments through metrics such as risk reduction, incident cost avoidance, and compliance achievement?

rubric.ad_hoc: No measurement of cybersecurity investment returns exists. Security spending is justified through compliance mandates rather than demonstrated value or risk reduction.
rubric.foundational: Basic metrics exist for some cybersecurity investments (e.g., number of incidents prevented, compliance scores achieved), but ROI is not systematically calculated or reported.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity ROI is measured through defined metrics including risk reduction (quantified exposure change), incident cost avoidance, and compliance achievement rates. ROI analysis informs budget requests and investment prioritization.
rubric.optimized: Comprehensive cybersecurity ROI measurement is continuous and automated. Multi-dimensional value metrics (risk reduction, operational efficiency, compliance, business enablement) are tracked at the investment level and aggregated for portfolio-level value reporting.

items.text: How effectively does the organization plan and budget for cybersecurity workforce development, including recruitment, retention, and training?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity workforce investment is not strategically planned. Recruitment and training budgets are ad hoc, and the organization struggles to compete for cybersecurity talent.
rubric.foundational: Basic cybersecurity workforce budgets exist, but strategic workforce planning connecting skills gaps to investment needs is limited. Training spending is not aligned with threat landscape evolution.
rubric.managed: Comprehensive cybersecurity workforce investment planning addresses recruitment, retention incentives (e.g., cyber pay), training, and certification aligned with NICE framework workforce categories. Spending is tracked against capability development goals.
rubric.optimized: Strategic cybersecurity workforce investment uses analytics to forecast talent needs, optimize the mix of federal, contractor, and managed security services, and measure ROI on workforce development. The organization is recognized as a cybersecurity employer of choice.

items.text: To what extent does the organization track and optimize spending on cybersecurity tools and technologies relative to threat coverage?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity tool spending is not analyzed for coverage effectiveness. Tools are acquired reactively in response to incidents or audit findings without portfolio-level analysis.
rubric.foundational: A cybersecurity tool inventory exists, but spending analysis does not assess coverage gaps, overlaps, or alignment with the threat landscape. Tool rationalization is not performed.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity tool spending is analyzed against a threat coverage framework (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK, NIST CSF). Coverage gaps and tool overlaps are identified, informing investment and rationalization decisions.
rubric.optimized: Advanced cybersecurity investment optimization maps tool capabilities to threat coverage, measures detection and prevention effectiveness per dollar spent, and continuously rationalizes the tool portfolio. Spending is benchmarked against risk profile and peer agencies.

items.text: How well does the organization align cybersecurity investments with Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM) program capabilities and CISA requirements?

rubric.ad_hoc: CDM program alignment is not considered in cybersecurity investment decisions. The organization may be duplicating CDM capabilities with agency-funded tools or missing CDM integration opportunities.
rubric.foundational: CDM capabilities are acknowledged, but investment planning does not systematically coordinate agency cybersecurity spending with CDM-provided capabilities. Gaps and overlaps between CDM and agency tools are not analyzed.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity investment planning coordinates with CDM program capabilities to avoid duplication and maximize coverage. Agency-funded investments complement CDM and align with CISA requirements and BOD/ED directives.
rubric.optimized: Fully integrated cybersecurity investment management optimizes spending across agency-funded and CDM-provided capabilities. The organization actively engages with CISA on CDM evolution and uses CDM data to inform risk-based investment decisions. Comprehensive coverage mapping demonstrates alignment with federal cybersecurity strategy.

items.text: How well does the organization invest in cybersecurity workforce development, including recruitment, retention, training, and skills assessment aligned with NICE framework?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity workforce development is ad hoc with no structured program for recruitment, retention, or skills development. Training is limited to basic awareness and compliance certifications.
rubric.foundational: Some cybersecurity training budget exists, but workforce development is not strategically planned. Recruitment challenges are addressed reactively, and retention strategies are limited. NICE framework alignment has not been assessed.
rubric.managed: A comprehensive cybersecurity workforce development program addresses recruitment, retention, training, and skills assessment. Work roles are mapped to the NICE framework, and skills gaps are systematically identified and addressed through targeted training and hiring.
rubric.optimized: Strategic cybersecurity workforce management includes competitive compensation strategies, career development pathways, rotation programs, and continuous skills assessment against NICE framework. The organization participates in CyberCorps and federal cyber reskilling initiatives, and workforce metrics are reported per FISMA requirements.

items.text: How effectively does the organization budget for and manage cybersecurity tool rationalization to avoid redundant capabilities and optimize security technology spending?

rubric.ad_hoc: Cybersecurity tools are acquired independently without portfolio-level visibility. Significant overlap in capabilities exists across tools, and total security tool spending is unknown.
rubric.foundational: A basic inventory of cybersecurity tools exists, but capability overlap analysis is not performed. Tool acquisition continues without assessing whether existing tools can meet the need.
rubric.managed: Cybersecurity tool portfolio is actively managed with capability mapping, overlap analysis, and utilization assessment. Tool rationalization reviews occur annually, and new acquisitions require justification against existing capabilities.
rubric.optimized: Optimized cybersecurity tool portfolio with automated capability mapping, integration scoring, and cost-effectiveness analysis. Rationalization is continuous, and the organization maximizes value from its security technology investments through consolidation, API integration, and platform strategies.

balanced.label: Balanced
balanced.description: Equal weight across all enabled disciplines. Weights are automatically recalculated when supplemental disciplines are toggled on or off.

custom.label: Custom
custom.description: User-defined weights for each enabled discipline. Weights must sum to 1.0.
